description: A clause that prohibits the employee from working for any competing business, across a broad geographic area (state-wide or pan-India), for an extended period (12+ months) after leaving the company. Often uses vague language like 'any business in the technology sector' without defining specific competitors.
why_risky: This pattern can effectively prevent you from earning a livelihood in your field for months or years after leaving. In India, such broad restrictions are generally unenforceable under Section 27 of the Indian Contract Act, but companies use them as scare tactics to discourage employees from leaving.
red_flag_phrases: any company in the technology sector
any business similar to the Company
what_to_look_for: Check three dimensions: (1) Duration — anything over 6 months is aggressive; (2) Geographic scope — pan-India is almost always unreasonable; (3) Competitor definition — vague definitions like 'technology sector' are overly broad.
negotiation_leverage: Section 27 of the Indian Contract Act renders restraint-of-trade agreements void. Indian courts (Pepsi Foods Ltd v. Bharat Coca-Cola, Wipro Ltd v. Beckman Coulter) have consistently refused to enforce broad non-compete clauses post-employment. You have strong legal backing to push back.
indian_law_reference: Section 27, Indian Contract Act, 1872
safer_alternative: Alternative: Check three dimensions: (1) Duration — anything over 6 months is aggressive; (2) Geographic scope — pan-India is almost always unreasonable; (3) Competitor definition — vague definitions like 'technology sector' are overly broad.
negotiation_script: I noticed the overly broad post-employment non-compete clause is quite broad. As per Section 27, Indian Contract Act, 1872, this is highly irregular. Could we revise this to be more balanced?

description: A non-compete clause with no specified end date, or one that lasts 'indefinitely' or 'in perpetuity' after the employment or engagement ends.
why_risky: A perpetual non-compete would permanently prevent you from working in your field. This is not just aggressive — it is plainly unenforceable under Indian law and signals that the company is using legal intimidation rather than legitimate business protection.
what_to_look_for: Look for the absence of a specific end date or phrases like 'at all times hereafter' or 'for an unlimited period.' Even clauses that say 'for 5 years' are extremely aggressive.
negotiation_leverage: No Indian court has ever enforced a perpetual non-compete. This clause is so clearly unenforceable that its inclusion suggests the employer is either using scare tactics or has poorly drafted contracts. Propose a 6-month non-solicitation as a reasonable alternative.
indian_law_reference: Section 27, Indian Contract Act, 1872
safer_alternative: Alternative: Look for the absence of a specific end date or phrases like 'at all times hereafter' or 'for an unlimited period.' Even clauses that say 'for 5 years' are extremely aggressive.
negotiation_script: I noticed the indefinite or perpetual non-compete clause is quite broad. As per Section 27, Indian Contract Act, 1872, this is highly irregular. Could we revise this to be more balanced?

description: A clause that prevents the individual from contacting or doing business with ANY client of the company, including clients they never worked with, for a long period after leaving.
why_risky: While non-solicitation of clients you directly worked with may be reasonable, a blanket prohibition on contacting ALL company clients — including those you never interacted with — is overly broad and restricts your ability to build your own business or career.
red_flag_phrases: any person who has been a client
what_to_look_for: Check if the clause limits non-solicitation to clients you directly worked with, or extends to ALL company clients. Also check the duration — over 12 months is aggressive for non-solicitation.
negotiation_leverage: Unlike non-compete, reasonable non-solicitation may be enforceable in India. However, courts require it to be reasonable in scope. Propose limiting it to clients you directly serviced in the last 12 months of your employment.
indian_law_reference: Section 27, Indian Contract Act, 1872 (courts distinguish non-solicitation from non-compete)
safer_alternative: Alternative: Check if the clause limits non-solicitation to clients you directly worked with, or extends to ALL company clients. Also check the duration — over 12 months is aggressive for non-solicitation.
negotiation_script: I noticed the overly broad non-solicitation of clients clause is quite broad. As per Section 27, Indian Contract Act, 1872 (courts distinguish non-solicitation from non-compete), this is highly irregular. Could we revise this to be more balanced?

description: A clause that allows the company to terminate the contract at any time, for any reason or no reason at all, with minimal or no notice — while the employee/contractor must provide longer notice or has restricted termination rights.
why_risky: One-sided termination rights create extreme job insecurity. If the company can fire you with 7 days' notice but you must give 90 days' notice, the relationship is fundamentally unbalanced. This power asymmetry can be used to exploit you — 'accept lower pay or we terminate.'
red_flag_phrases: at the sole discretion of the Company
what_to_look_for: Compare the notice period the company gives you vs. what you must give them. Check if termination-for-convenience is available to both parties equally. Look for phrases like 'at the sole discretion of the Management.'
negotiation_leverage: Push for symmetry — if the company can terminate with 30 days' notice, you should also be able to leave with 30 days' notice. For employees classified as 'workmen,' Section 25F of the Industrial Disputes Act requires one month's notice and retrenchment compensation.
indian_law_reference: Section 25F, Industrial Disputes Act, 1947; General contract law principles of mutuality
safer_alternative: Alternative: Compare the notice period the company gives you vs. what you must give them. Check if termination-for-convenience is available to both parties equally. Look for phrases like 'at the sole discretion of the Management.'
negotiation_script: I noticed the termination without cause — company only clause is quite broad. As per Section 25F, Industrial Disputes Act, 1947; General contract law principles of mutuality, this is highly irregular. Could we revise this to be more balanced?

description: A clause where the employee must give significantly longer notice (60-90 days) than the company (15-30 days), or where the company has the right to extend the employee's notice period unilaterally.
why_risky: Long notice periods trap employees — a 90-day notice makes it very difficult to switch jobs because most new employers won't wait 3 months. Some companies also refuse to accept 'payment in lieu of notice' (buying out the notice period), further trapping the employee.
red_flag_phrases: notice period cannot be bought out
what_to_look_for: Check: (1) Is the notice period the same for both parties? (2) Can you buy out the notice by paying salary in lieu? (3) Can the company unilaterally extend your notice? (4) What happens if you leave without serving full notice?
negotiation_leverage: In the IT industry, 30-60 days is standard. Push for: (1) equal notice periods, (2) explicit right to buy out notice, and (3) no company discretion to extend notice. State labor laws (Shops & Establishments Acts) often cap notice at 30 days.
indian_law_reference: State Shops & Establishments Acts; ICA Section 73 (compensation for actual loss only)
safer_alternative: Alternative: Check: (1) Is the notice period the same for both parties? (2) Can you buy out the notice by paying salary in lieu? (3) Can the company unilaterally extend your notice? (4) What happens if you leave without serving full notice?
negotiation_script: I noticed the asymmetric notice period clause is quite broad. As per State Shops & Establishments Acts; ICA Section 73 (compensation for actual loss only), this is highly irregular. Could we revise this to be more balanced?

description: A clause that assigns ALL intellectual property created by the employee to the company — not just work done during office hours on company projects, but also personal projects, side projects, open-source contributions, and inventions created on personal time.
why_risky: An overly broad IP clause can claim ownership over your personal projects, your blog posts, your open-source contributions, or even a startup idea you develop on weekends. This effectively takes ownership of your entire creative output.
red_flag_phrases: whether or not created during working hours
regardless of when or where created
what_to_look_for: Check if the clause limits IP assignment to: (1) work done during working hours, (2) using company resources, (3) related to the company's business. If any of these limits are missing, the clause is too broad.
negotiation_leverage: Push for IP assignment limited to work: (1) done during working hours AND (2) using company resources AND (3) directly related to the company's current business. Explicitly carve out personal projects, open-source contributions, and pre-existing IP.
indian_law_reference: Section 17, Indian Copyright Act, 1957 (employer is first owner of work done in course of employment)
safer_alternative: Alternative: Check if the clause limits IP assignment to: (1) work done during working hours, (2) using company resources, (3) related to the company's business. If any of these limits are missing, the clause is too broad.
negotiation_script: I noticed the overly broad ip assignment clause is quite broad. As per Section 17, Indian Copyright Act, 1957 (employer is first owner of work done in course of employment), this is highly irregular. Could we revise this to be more balanced?

description: A clause that transfers ownership of the freelancer's pre-existing intellectual property (tools, frameworks, code libraries, methodologies) to the client simply because they were used in the project.
why_risky: If you use your own tools or frameworks to deliver a project, this clause would transfer ownership of YOUR tools to the client. You could lose rights to software or methods you developed over years, just because you used them on one project.
red_flag_phrases: all materials used in connection with
what_to_look_for: Check if the contract distinguishes between 'project deliverables' (which the client should own) and 'pre-existing IP' (which should remain yours). Look for a 'Background IP' or 'Prior Inventions' schedule.
negotiation_leverage: Insist on a clear separation: (1) New work created specifically for this project = client owns it, (2) Your pre-existing tools/frameworks = you retain ownership with a license granted to the client. Attach a 'Prior Inventions Schedule' listing your pre-existing IP.
indian_law_reference: Section 18-19, Indian Copyright Act, 1957 (assignment must be voluntary and specific)
safer_alternative: Alternative: Check if the contract distinguishes between 'project deliverables' (which the client should own) and 'pre-existing IP' (which should remain yours). Look for a 'Background IP' or 'Prior Inventions' schedule.
negotiation_script: I noticed the pre-existing ip capture clause is quite broad. As per Section 18-19, Indian Copyright Act, 1957 (assignment must be voluntary and specific), this is highly irregular. Could we revise this to be more balanced?

description: A clause that allows the client to pay 45-90+ days after invoice submission, or ties payment to vague milestones like 'client satisfaction' or 'project acceptance' without clear acceptance criteria.
why_risky: Delayed payment terms create cash flow problems for freelancers and small businesses. When payment is tied to subjective criteria like 'satisfaction,' the client can delay indefinitely by claiming dissatisfaction. Net-90 terms effectively give the client a 3-month interest-free loan at your expense.
red_flag_phrases: at the sole discretion of payment
what_to_look_for: Check: (1) How many days after invoice? Anything over Net-30 is aggressive for freelancers; (2) Are payment milestones clearly defined? (3) Is there a penalty for late payment? (4) What happens if the client disputes the invoice?
negotiation_leverage: Standard freelance payment is Net-15 to Net-30. Push for: (1) 50% advance + 50% on delivery, (2) Clear acceptance criteria with a 5-7 day acceptance window (silence = acceptance), (3) Late payment interest (1-2% per month). Payment of Wages Act mandates salary within 7-10 days for employees.
indian_law_reference: Section 5, Payment of Wages Act, 1936 (for employees); General contract law for freelancers
safer_alternative: Alternative: Check: (1) How many days after invoice? Anything over Net-30 is aggressive for freelancers; (2) Are payment milestones clearly defined? (3) Is there a penalty for late payment? (4) What happens if the client disputes the invoice?
negotiation_script: I noticed the excessively delayed payment terms clause is quite broad. As per Section 5, Payment of Wages Act, 1936 (for employees); General contract law for freelancers, this is highly irregular. Could we revise this to be more balanced?

pattern_name: Discretionary Bonus / Variable Pay Without Criteria
description: A clause where a significant portion of total compensation (30-50%) is labeled as 'discretionary bonus,' 'variable pay,' or 'performance incentive' with criteria determined 'at the sole discretion of management.'
why_risky: If 40% of your CTC is 'variable' with no clear criteria, the company can effectively cut your pay by 40% at any time. Phrases like 'at the sole discretion of management' mean the company has no contractual obligation to pay the variable component.
red_flag_phrases: at the sole discretion of management
variable pay subject to company performance
what_to_look_for: Calculate what percentage of your total CTC is guaranteed (fixed) vs. variable. If variable is over 20-30%, demand clear, measurable criteria for earning it. Check if the variable component has been historically paid to other employees.
negotiation_leverage: Push for: (1) Clear, measurable performance criteria for variable pay, (2) Historical data on variable pay disbursement, (3) A minimum guarantee of at least 70-80% of CTC as fixed. If possible, negotiate a higher fixed component even if the total CTC is slightly lower.
indian_law_reference: Section 7, Payment of Wages Act, 1936 (authorized deductions); General principles of contract certainty
safer_alternative: Alternative: Calculate what percentage of your total CTC is guaranteed (fixed) vs. variable. If variable is over 20-30%, demand clear, measurable criteria for earning it. Check if the variable component has been historically paid to other employees.
negotiation_script: I noticed the discretionary bonus / variable pay without criteria clause is quite broad. As per Section 7, Payment of Wages Act, 1936 (authorized deductions); General principles of contract certainty, this is highly irregular. Could we revise this to be more balanced?

description: A clause that makes the individual personally liable for unlimited damages, including consequential, indirect, and special damages, without any cap on total liability.
why_risky: Uncapped liability means you could owe the client millions of rupees if something goes wrong — even if your project fee was only ₹50,000. Without a cap, a single mistake could bankrupt you. This is especially dangerous for freelancers and small businesses without insurance.
red_flag_phrases: liable for any and all losses
including but not limited to indirect damages
what_to_look_for: Look for: (1) Is there a liability cap? (2) Are consequential/indirect damages excluded? (3) Is liability mutual (both parties) or one-sided? Standard practice is to cap liability at 1-2x the total contract value.
negotiation_leverage: Industry standard is liability capped at 1x the total contract value, excluding consequential damages. Push for: (1) Liability cap = total project value, (2) Exclusion of indirect/consequential damages, (3) Mutual limitation (applies to both parties).
indian_law_reference: Section 73, Indian Contract Act, 1872 (compensation for direct loss only; remote damages excluded)
safer_alternative: Alternative: Look for: (1) Is there a liability cap? (2) Are consequential/indirect damages excluded? (3) Is liability mutual (both parties) or one-sided? Standard practice is to cap liability at 1-2x the total contract value.
negotiation_script: I noticed the uncapped personal liability clause is quite broad. As per Section 73, Indian Contract Act, 1872 (compensation for direct loss only; remote damages excluded), this is highly irregular. Could we revise this to be more balanced?

description: A clause where only the individual indemnifies the company (covers their losses), but the company does not indemnify the individual. The individual bears the cost of defending the company against third-party claims, legal fees, and damages.
why_risky: One-sided indemnity means you bear the financial risk of lawsuits against the company, even if the company's own actions contributed to the problem. You could be forced to pay legal fees and damages that exceed your entire earnings from the contract.
red_flag_phrases: Contractor shall indemnify and hold harmless the Company
including legal fees and court costs
what_to_look_for: Check: (1) Is indemnification mutual? (2) Is the scope of indemnification reasonable (only your negligence/breach) or overly broad (any claim 'arising out of' the work)? (3) Is there a cap on indemnification costs? (4) Does the company indemnify you for their negligence?
negotiation_leverage: Push for: (1) Mutual indemnification — both parties indemnify each other, (2) Limited scope — only for claims arising from your actual negligence or breach, not every possible claim, (3) Cap on indemnification costs, (4) Company must indemnify you against claims arising from their instructions or materials.
indian_law_reference: Sections 124-125, Indian Contract Act, 1872 (Contract of Indemnity); Section 73 (proportional damages)
safer_alternative: Alternative: Check: (1) Is indemnification mutual? (2) Is the scope of indemnification reasonable (only your negligence/breach) or overly broad (any claim 'arising out of' the work)? (3) Is there a cap on indemnification costs? (4) Does the company indemnify you for their negligence?
negotiation_script: I noticed the one-sided indemnification clause is quite broad. As per Sections 124-125, Indian Contract Act, 1872 (Contract of Indemnity); Section 73 (proportional damages), this is highly irregular. Could we revise this to be more balanced?

description: A confidentiality clause with no end date, or one that lasts 'indefinitely' or 'in perpetuity' after the engagement ends, combined with an overly broad definition of 'confidential information.'
why_risky: A perpetual NDA with a broad definition means virtually everything you learn or work on becomes permanently off-limits. You can't discuss your work experience in future job interviews, can't mention the client's name, and can't use general skills or knowledge gained during the engagement.
red_flag_phrases: any information relating to the Company's business
what_to_look_for: Check: (1) Is there a specific end date? (2-5 years after termination is standard); (2) What is considered 'confidential'? Does it include general knowledge and skills? (3) Are there carve-outs for publicly available information? (4) Can you discuss your work experience in job interviews?
negotiation_leverage: Standard NDA duration is 2-3 years after termination. Push for: (1) Specific end date, (2) Clear definition of what is and isn't confidential, (3) Explicit carve-outs for general skills, publicly available info, and information you already knew, (4) Right to identify the client and describe your role (without specifics) in your portfolio.
indian_law_reference: General contract law; Section 27, ICA 1872 (if NDA effectively restrains trade)
safer_alternative: Alternative: Check: (1) Is there a specific end date? (2-5 years after termination is standard); (2) What is considered 'confidential'? Does it include general knowledge and skills? (3) Are there carve-outs for publicly available information? (4) Can you discuss your work experience in job interviews?
negotiation_script: I noticed the perpetual or excessively long nda clause is quite broad. As per General contract law; Section 27, ICA 1872 (if NDA effectively restrains trade), this is highly irregular. Could we revise this to be more balanced?

description: A confidentiality clause that defines 'confidential information' so broadly that it encompasses virtually all information the employee encounters — including general industry knowledge, publicly available data, and skills learned on the job.
why_risky: When everything is 'confidential,' the distinction between proprietary company secrets and your general professional knowledge disappears. This effectively prevents you from using skills and knowledge you developed, limiting your career growth and employability.
red_flag_phrases: whether or not marked as confidential
any information relating to the business
what_to_look_for: A good confidentiality clause defines specific categories of confidential information (client lists, pricing, source code, trade secrets). A bad one says 'all information' is confidential. Also look for carve-outs — the clause should exclude: publicly available info, info you already knew, info independently developed, and general skills/knowledge.
negotiation_leverage: Push for: (1) Specific categories of confidential information instead of 'everything,' (2) Standard carve-outs for public info, prior knowledge, and independent development, (3) Explicit acknowledgment that general skills and industry knowledge are NOT confidential.
indian_law_reference: General contract law; judicial interpretation of 'reasonable' confidentiality obligations
safer_alternative: Alternative: A good confidentiality clause defines specific categories of confidential information (client lists, pricing, source code, trade secrets). A bad one says 'all information' is confidential. Also look for carve-outs — the clause should exclude: publicly available info, info you already knew, info independently developed, and general skills/knowledge.
negotiation_script: I noticed the overly broad confidentiality definition clause is quite broad. As per General contract law; judicial interpretation of 'reasonable' confidentiality obligations, this is highly irregular. Could we revise this to be more balanced?

description: An employment bond that requires the employee to pay a large sum (₹2-10+ lakh) if they leave before a specified period (1-3 years), but the actual training provided is minimal — often just standard onboarding or induction.
why_risky: Disproportionate bonds are used to trap employees. If a company charges ₹5 lakh for a 2-month orientation that cost them ₹30,000, the bond amount is grossly inflated. However, many freshers comply out of fear, not knowing that courts regularly reduce such penalties to actual costs.
what_to_look_for: Check: (1) What specific training is the company providing? (Standard onboarding doesn't justify a bond); (2) What is the actual cost of the training? (Ask for documentation); (3) Is the bond amount proportional to the training cost? (4) How long is the lock-in period? More than 1 year for standard training is excessive.
negotiation_leverage: Under Section 74 of ICA, courts only award reasonable compensation — not the full penalty amount. Key cases: SICPA India v. Manu Anand (Delhi HC) held that bond amount must be proportional to actual training expenditure. ONGC v. Saw Pipes (SC) established that only reasonable compensation can be awarded. Additionally, Section 14 of the Specific Relief Act means courts cannot force you to continue working — only monetary damages can be claimed.
indian_law_reference: Section 74, Indian Contract Act, 1872; Section 14, Specific Relief Act, 1963
safer_alternative: Alternative: Check: (1) What specific training is the company providing? (Standard onboarding doesn't justify a bond); (2) What is the actual cost of the training? (Ask for documentation); (3) Is the bond amount proportional to the training cost? (4) How long is the lock-in period? More than 1 year for standard training is excessive.
negotiation_script: I noticed the disproportionate training bond amount clause is quite broad. As per Section 74, Indian Contract Act, 1872; Section 14, Specific Relief Act, 1963, this is highly irregular. Could we revise this to be more balanced?

description: A bond or service agreement that requires the employee to stay for an unreasonably long period (2-5+ years) relative to the training or investment the company has made.
why_risky: A 3-year lock-in for a company that provided 2 weeks of standard orientation is exploitative. Long lock-in periods prevent career growth, salary increases through job changes, and trap employees in unfavorable working conditions.
red_flag_phrases: shall serve for a period of not less than
what_to_look_for: Evaluate proportionality: (1) What training was provided? (2) How long did the training last? (3) Is the lock-in period proportional to the training investment? A rule of thumb: 3-6 months of genuine training might justify a 1-year bond; standard onboarding justifies nothing.
negotiation_leverage: Courts apply a proportionality test. A 3-year bond for 2 weeks of induction is clearly disproportionate. Push for: (1) Reduce lock-in to match training duration, (2) Pro-rata reduction of penalty as you serve more time, (3) Explicit right to buy out the bond at a reduced rate after partial service.
indian_law_reference: Section 74, Indian Contract Act, 1872; SICPA India v. Manu Anand (Delhi HC)
safer_alternative: Alternative: Evaluate proportionality: (1) What training was provided? (2) How long did the training last? (3) Is the lock-in period proportional to the training investment? A rule of thumb: 3-6 months of genuine training might justify a 1-year bond; standard onboarding justifies nothing.
negotiation_script: I noticed the unreasonable lock-in period clause is quite broad. As per Section 74, Indian Contract Act, 1872; SICPA India v. Manu Anand (Delhi HC), this is highly irregular. Could we revise this to be more balanced?

description: An arbitration clause where: (1) the company unilaterally selects the arbitrator, (2) arbitration must happen in a distant city inconvenient for the individual, (3) the individual bears all arbitration costs, or (4) the arbitration decision is binding but appeal rights are waived.
why_risky: Biased arbitration can deny you a fair hearing. If the company picks the arbitrator, the arbitrator has an incentive to rule in the company's favor to get future business. If arbitration is in a distant city, the cost and inconvenience may prevent you from pursuing your claim.
red_flag_phrases: costs to be borne by the Employee
sole arbitrator nominated by the Company
what_to_look_for: Check: (1) Who appoints the arbitrator? (Should be mutual agreement or an independent body); (2) Where is the arbitration venue? (Should be convenient for you); (3) Who pays the costs? (Should be shared or borne by the losing party); (4) Can you appeal the decision?
negotiation_leverage: The Supreme Court in Perkins Eastman Architects v. HSCC (2019) held that unilateral arbitrator appointment by one party is invalid. Push for: (1) Mutual appointment or appointment by an independent body (like the Indian Council of Arbitration), (2) Arbitration at a mutually convenient location, (3) Shared costs. For consumer disputes, consumer commission jurisdiction cannot be ousted by arbitration.
indian_law_reference: Section 7 & 11, Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996; Perkins Eastman v. HSCC (2019 SC)
safer_alternative: Alternative: Check: (1) Who appoints the arbitrator? (Should be mutual agreement or an independent body); (2) Where is the arbitration venue? (Should be convenient for you); (3) Who pays the costs? (Should be shared or borne by the losing party); (4) Can you appeal the decision?
negotiation_script: I noticed the biased arbitration clause clause is quite broad. As per Section 7 & 11, Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996; Perkins Eastman v. HSCC (2019 SC), this is highly irregular. Could we revise this to be more balanced?

description: A clause that requires all legal disputes to be filed exclusively in courts of a specific city that is far from where the individual lives or works, making it impractical for them to pursue legal action.
why_risky: If you live in Chennai but the contract says 'exclusive jurisdiction of courts in Mumbai,' you would need to hire a Mumbai lawyer, travel to Mumbai for hearings, and bear the cost of litigating in a distant city. This effectively denies access to justice for smaller claims.
red_flag_phrases: only courts at [city] shall have jurisdiction
disputes shall be subject to the jurisdiction of
what_to_look_for: Check if the jurisdiction is 'exclusive' (you can ONLY file there) or 'non-exclusive' (you can also file where you live). Non-exclusive jurisdiction clauses are fairer. Also check if the city is practical for you.
negotiation_leverage: Push for 'non-exclusive jurisdiction' so you can also file in courts where you live. For consumer contracts, consumer commission jurisdiction cannot be ousted (CPA 2019, Section 47). For employment disputes, the location of employment is typically the appropriate jurisdiction.
indian_law_reference: Section 28, Indian Contract Act, 1872; Section 47, Consumer Protection Act, 2019; Section 20, CPC (cause of action jurisdiction)
safer_alternative: Alternative: Check if the jurisdiction is 'exclusive' (you can ONLY file there) or 'non-exclusive' (you can also file where you live). Non-exclusive jurisdiction clauses are fairer. Also check if the city is practical for you.
negotiation_script: I noticed the inconvenient exclusive jurisdiction clause is quite broad. As per Section 28, Indian Contract Act, 1872; Section 47, Consumer Protection Act, 2019; Section 20, CPC (cause of action jurisdiction), this is highly irregular. Could we revise this to be more balanced?

description: A clause that automatically renews the contract for another full term unless the individual sends a cancellation notice 30-90 days before the renewal date, often buried deep in the terms and conditions.
why_risky: Auto-renewal traps consumers into paying for services they no longer want. If you forget to cancel within the narrow window, you're locked in for another year. Some companies make it easy to sign up but deliberately difficult to cancel (dark patterns).
red_flag_phrases: unless written notice is provided 60 days prior
renewal shall be at the then-current rates
deemed to have agreed to renewal
what_to_look_for: Check: (1) How far in advance must you notify to cancel? (More than 30 days is aggressive); (2) Is there a clear cancellation mechanism? (3) Does the renewal happen at the same or increased price? (4) Are you notified before renewal?
negotiation_leverage: Under Consumer Protection Act 2019, Section 2(46), unfair auto-renewal terms can be challenged. Push for: (1) 15-day cancellation notice maximum, (2) Proactive renewal notification from the company 30 days before, (3) Easy cancellation mechanism (online, not just registered post), (4) Price lock during renewal.
indian_law_reference: Section 2(46), Consumer Protection Act, 2019 (unfair contract terms)
safer_alternative: Alternative: Check: (1) How far in advance must you notify to cancel? (More than 30 days is aggressive); (2) Is there a clear cancellation mechanism? (3) Does the renewal happen at the same or increased price? (4) Are you notified before renewal?
negotiation_script: I noticed the auto-renewal without adequate notice clause is quite broad. As per Section 2(46), Consumer Protection Act, 2019 (unfair contract terms), this is highly irregular. Could we revise this to be more balanced?

description: A clause that grants the company blanket consent to collect, process, store, and share your personal data (including sensitive data like biometrics, health records, financial information) without specifying what data, why, how long, or with whom.
why_risky: Blanket data consent gives the company unlimited rights over your personal information. They could share your salary details with third parties, use your biometric data for surveillance, or sell your personal information — all with your 'consent.'
red_flag_phrases: consent to collection and processing of all personal data
including but not limited to biometric data
may share with third parties at Company's discretion
what_to_look_for: Check: (1) What specific data is collected? (2) Why is it needed? (3) How long is it stored? (4) Who can access it? (5) Can you withdraw consent? (6) What happens to your data when you leave? Companies must comply with IT Act Section 43A and the Information Technology (Reasonable Security Practices and Procedures) Rules, 2011.
negotiation_leverage: Under IT Act Section 43A, companies must implement reasonable security practices for sensitive personal data. Push for: (1) Specific data inventory, (2) Purpose limitation, (3) Data deletion upon departure, (4) Right to withdraw consent, (5) Data breach notification requirement.
indian_law_reference: Section 43A, Information Technology Act, 2000; IT (Reasonable Security Practices) Rules, 2011
safer_alternative: Alternative: Check: (1) What specific data is collected? (2) Why is it needed? (3) How long is it stored? (4) Who can access it? (5) Can you withdraw consent? (6) What happens to your data when you leave? Companies must comply with IT Act Section 43A and the Information Technology (Reasonable Security Practices and Procedures) Rules, 2011.
negotiation_script: I noticed the overly broad personal data collection clause is quite broad. As per Section 43A, Information Technology Act, 2000; IT (Reasonable Security Practices) Rules, 2011, this is highly irregular. Could we revise this to be more balanced?

description: A clause that allows the client to change the scope of work, add requirements, or modify deliverables at any time without renegotiating the price or timeline.
why_risky: Scope creep is the #1 killer of freelance profitability. If the client can add unlimited requirements without paying more, you end up doing 3x the work for the original price. This effectively reduces your hourly rate to a fraction of what you agreed.
red_flag_phrases: Company may modify the scope at any time
as reasonably requested by the Company
what_to_look_for: Check: (1) Is the scope of work clearly defined? (2) What is the change request process? (3) Are additional requirements subject to additional payment? (4) Can you reject unreasonable changes? (5) Is there a maximum number of revision rounds?
negotiation_leverage: Push for: (1) Clearly defined scope of work as an attached schedule, (2) Formal change request process for any modifications, (3) Additional compensation for additional work, (4) Right to renegotiate timeline for scope changes, (5) Maximum 2-3 revision rounds included in the price.
indian_law_reference: General contract law — consideration must be present for contract modification (ICA Section 10)
safer_alternative: Alternative: Check: (1) Is the scope of work clearly defined? (2) What is the change request process? (3) Are additional requirements subject to additional payment? (4) Can you reject unreasonable changes? (5) Is there a maximum number of revision rounds?
negotiation_script: I noticed the unilateral scope change / modification clause clause is quite broad. As per General contract law — consideration must be present for contract modification (ICA Section 10), this is highly irregular. Could we revise this to be more balanced?

description: A clause that allows the company to withhold final settlement (last salary, leave encashment, gratuity, PF) for 60-90+ days after the last working day, or makes it conditional on returning all 'company property' (defined vaguely).
why_risky: Delayed final settlement can leave you without income for months after leaving a job. When it's conditional on vaguely defined 'company property,' the employer can delay indefinitely by claiming you haven't returned everything. Some companies use this as leverage to force signing of NDAs or non-compete extensions.
red_flag_phrases: full and final settlement within 90 days
subject to return of all company property
after completion of all exit formalities
what_to_look_for: Check: (1) When is full and final settlement due? (30 days maximum is reasonable); (2) Is it conditional on vague terms? (3) Does it include relieving letter and experience certificate? (4) What constitutes 'company property'? (5) Can you dispute any deductions?
negotiation_leverage: Under Payment of Wages Act, wages must be paid within 7-10 days. Withholding salary, PF, or gratuity as leverage is legally questionable. Push for: (1) Settlement within 30 days, (2) Clear list of company property, (3) Automatic release of relieving letter on last day, (4) No unilateral deductions from settlement.
indian_law_reference: Section 5, Payment of Wages Act, 1936; Payment of Gratuity Act, 1972; EPF Act
safer_alternative: Alternative: Check: (1) When is full and final settlement due? (30 days maximum is reasonable); (2) Is it conditional on vague terms? (3) Does it include relieving letter and experience certificate? (4) What constitutes 'company property'? (5) Can you dispute any deductions?
negotiation_script: I noticed the delayed or conditional final settlement clause is quite broad. As per Section 5, Payment of Wages Act, 1936; Payment of Gratuity Act, 1972; EPF Act, this is highly irregular. Could we revise this to be more balanced?

pattern_name: Inclusion of Consequential and Indirect Damages
description: A clause that makes the individual liable for 'consequential,' 'indirect,' 'incidental,' or 'special' damages — i.e., downstream losses that the client claims were caused by the individual's work, even if they are far removed from the actual deliverable.
why_risky: Consequential damages can be astronomical and unpredictable. If your website design has a bug that causes the client to lose sales, they could claim millions in 'lost revenue' as consequential damages — even if your design fee was ₹1 lakh.
what_to_look_for: Check if the contract explicitly EXCLUDES consequential and indirect damages. Standard practice is a mutual exclusion clause: 'Neither party shall be liable for consequential, indirect, incidental, or special damages.' If this exclusion is absent, you are exposed to unlimited downstream liability.
negotiation_leverage: Section 73 of the ICA already limits compensation to 'direct and natural' consequences of breach. Remote and indirect damages are excluded by statute. Push for: (1) Explicit exclusion of consequential damages, (2) Liability cap, (3) Both exclusion and cap should be mutual.
indian_law_reference: Section 73, Indian Contract Act, 1872 (compensation for direct loss only)
safer_alternative: Alternative: Check if the contract explicitly EXCLUDES consequential and indirect damages. Standard practice is a mutual exclusion clause: 'Neither party shall be liable for consequential, indirect, incidental, or special damages.' If this exclusion is absent, you are exposed to unlimited downstream liability.
negotiation_script: I noticed the inclusion of consequential and indirect damages clause is quite broad. As per Section 73, Indian Contract Act, 1872 (compensation for direct loss only), this is highly irregular. Could we revise this to be more balanced?

description: A clause that allows the company to assign or transfer the contract (and your obligations) to any third party without your knowledge or consent, while you cannot assign your rights.
why_risky: Your contract could be transferred to a completely different company — one you never agreed to work with. They might be in a different city, have different payment practices, or impose additional requirements. You signed up to work with Company A, but now you're obligated to Company B.
red_flag_phrases: Company may assign this agreement without consent
transfer to any affiliate, subsidiary, or successor
Contractor may not assign without prior written consent
what_to_look_for: Check if: (1) The company can assign freely but you cannot, (2) Assignment includes all your obligations to the new party, (3) The new party must honor the same terms, (4) You have the right to terminate if the contract is assigned.
negotiation_leverage: Push for: (1) Mutual consent for assignment — both parties need the other's approval, (2) If the company assigns, you have the right to terminate without penalty, (3) The assignee must accept all existing terms (no renegotiation to your disadvantage).
indian_law_reference: General contract law — assignment requires consent for personal service contracts
safer_alternative: Alternative: Check if: (1) The company can assign freely but you cannot, (2) Assignment includes all your obligations to the new party, (3) The new party must honor the same terms, (4) You have the right to terminate if the contract is assigned.
negotiation_script: I noticed the assignment without consent clause is quite broad. As per General contract law — assignment requires consent for personal service contracts, this is highly irregular. Could we revise this to be more balanced?

description: A clause that imposes a heavy financial penalty for cancelling a consumer contract early — such as paying the entire remaining contract value, forfeiting deposits, or paying a flat 'early exit fee' that is disproportionate to any actual loss.
why_risky: Excessive exit penalties trap consumers in contracts they no longer want or need. If cancelling a ₹12,000/year subscription costs ₹8,000 in 'early termination fees,' the penalty is clearly disproportionate.
what_to_look_for: Check: (1) What is the exit penalty amount? (2) Is it proportional to any actual loss the company suffers? (3) Can you cancel within a cooling-off period without penalty? (4) Does the penalty decrease as you use more of the contract term?
negotiation_leverage: Under CPA 2019 Section 2(46), disproportionate penalties are an 'unfair contract' term. Push for: (1) Pro-rata charges only (pay for what you used), (2) A cooling-off period (7-15 days after signing), (3) Penalty that decreases over the contract term, (4) No penalty if the company breaches first.
indian_law_reference: Section 2(46), Consumer Protection Act, 2019; Section 74, Indian Contract Act, 1872
safer_alternative: Alternative: Check: (1) What is the exit penalty amount? (2) Is it proportional to any actual loss the company suffers? (3) Can you cancel within a cooling-off period without penalty? (4) Does the penalty decrease as you use more of the contract term?
negotiation_script: I noticed the excessive early termination penalty (consumer) clause is quite broad. As per Section 2(46), Consumer Protection Act, 2019; Section 74, Indian Contract Act, 1872, this is highly irregular. Could we revise this to be more balanced?

description: A force majeure clause that excuses only the company from performance during extraordinary events (pandemic, natural disaster, government orders) while requiring the individual to continue performing regardless.
why_risky: If a pandemic or natural disaster affects YOUR ability to deliver, but the clause only protects the company, you could be held liable for non-performance during an event that equally affected you. One-sided force majeure creates unfair risk allocation.
red_flag_phrases: Company shall not be liable for delays caused by
Contractor shall continue performance regardless of
force majeure events shall not excuse Contractor's obligations
what_to_look_for: Check: (1) Does force majeure apply to BOTH parties? (2) What events are covered? (3) What are the obligations during a force majeure event? (4) Can either party terminate if the event continues beyond a certain period?
negotiation_leverage: Section 56 of the ICA already provides for impossibility of performance. Push for: (1) Mutual force majeure — both parties are excused, (2) Clear definition of qualifying events, (3) Right to terminate if force majeure continues beyond 30-60 days, (4) No penalty for non-performance during force majeure.
indian_law_reference: Section 56, Indian Contract Act, 1872 (doctrine of frustration)
safer_alternative: Alternative: Check: (1) Does force majeure apply to BOTH parties? (2) What events are covered? (3) What are the obligations during a force majeure event? (4) Can either party terminate if the event continues beyond a certain period?
negotiation_script: I noticed the one-sided force majeure clause clause is quite broad. As per Section 56, Indian Contract Act, 1872 (doctrine of frustration), this is highly irregular. Could we revise this to be more balanced?

description: Clauses that require employees to repay exorbitant training costs if they leave before a certain period.

what_to_look_for: Check if the training costs are justified and proportionate to the training provided.
safer_alternative: Limit recovery to reasonable costs and ensure training is beneficial to the employee's role.
negotiation_leverage: Highlight the lack of proportionality and potential unenforceability.

description: An IP assignment clause that assigns all intellectual property created by the employee during the term of employment, regardless of its relevance to the employer's business.
why_risky: Such clauses can lead to disputes over ownership of personal projects and may be unenforceable if deemed unreasonable under Indian law.
what_to_look_for: Clauses that do not limit IP assignment to work-related inventions.
safer_alternative: Limit IP assignment to inventions directly related to the employer's business.
negotiation_leverage: Highlight potential unenforceability and unfairness under Indian law.
negotiation_script: I would like to discuss narrowing the IP assignment clause to cover only work-related inventions to ensure fairness and compliance with Indian legal standards.
indian_law_reference: Indian Contract Act, 1872 - Section 27

description: A clause that requires freelancers to assign all IP created during the contract period, without specifying the scope or relevance to the project.
why_risky: Freelancers may lose rights to unrelated personal projects, leading to potential legal challenges.
what_to_look_for: Clauses that do not specify the scope of IP assignment to project-related work.
safer_alternative: Specify that IP assignment is limited to deliverables outlined in the contract.
negotiation_leverage: Argue for fairness and protection of personal IP rights.
negotiation_script: I propose we limit the IP assignment to the specific deliverables of this project to protect both parties' interests.
indian_law_reference: Indian Copyright Act, 1957

description: An IP assignment clause in investment agreements that assigns all current and future IP to investors.
why_risky: Founders may lose control over core IP, affecting business autonomy and future funding rounds.
what_to_look_for: Clauses that do not reserve rights for the startup to use its own IP.
safer_alternative: Negotiate for a license-back arrangement or limit assignment to specific IP.
negotiation_leverage: Emphasize the importance of retaining IP for operational control and growth.
negotiation_script: To ensure our startup's growth and operational control, we propose a license-back arrangement for the IP assigned.
indian_law_reference: Indian Companies Act, 2013

pattern_name: Consultant IP Assignment Without Project Scope
description: A clause that requires consultants to assign all IP created during the consultancy, without linking it to specific project deliverables.
why_risky: Consultants may inadvertently assign unrelated IP, leading to potential loss of proprietary methods or tools.
what_to_look_for: Clauses that do not tie IP assignment to specific project outcomes.
safer_alternative: Limit IP assignment to project-specific deliverables.
negotiation_leverage: Point out the need for clarity and fairness in IP ownership.
negotiation_script: For clarity and fairness, let's limit the IP assignment to the specific deliverables of this consultancy project.
indian_law_reference: Indian Contract Act, 1872

pattern_name: Broad IP Assignment in Partnership Agreements
description: An IP assignment clause in partnership agreements that assigns all IP developed during the partnership to one partner.
why_risky: This can lead to disputes and imbalance in the partnership, especially if the IP is jointly developed.
what_to_look_for: Clauses that do not provide for joint ownership of jointly developed IP.
safer_alternative: Negotiate for joint ownership or a fair distribution of IP rights.
negotiation_leverage: Emphasize the collaborative nature of the partnership and the need for equitable IP distribution.
negotiation_script: Given the collaborative nature of our partnership, I suggest we establish joint ownership of any IP developed together.
indian_law_reference: Indian Partnership Act, 1932

description: Assignment clause that requires the transfer of all intellectual property rights, including those unrelated to the work performed under the contract.
why_risky: Such clauses can lead to the loss of rights over unrelated personal projects or inventions, potentially stifling innovation and personal growth.
what_to_look_for: Clauses that do not limit the assignment to work created during the term of employment or specifically for the employer.
safer_alternative: Limit the assignment to IP created during the course of employment and directly related to the employer's business.
negotiation_leverage: Highlight the importance of personal projects for skill development and innovation.
negotiation_script: I would like to ensure that the IP assignment is limited to work directly related to my role and created during my employment.
indian_law_reference: Indian Contract Act, 1872 - Section 27 (restraint of trade)

description: IP assignment clauses that require the transfer of all rights to any IP developed during the contract period, without specifying relevance to the project.
why_risky: This can result in the loss of rights to IP that is not related to the contracted work, affecting future business opportunities.
what_to_look_for: Clauses that do not specify the scope of IP related to the contracted work.
safer_alternative: Specify that only IP directly related to the contracted project is assigned.
negotiation_leverage: Emphasize the need to protect unrelated IP for future projects and collaborations.
negotiation_script: Can we specify that the IP assignment is limited to work directly related to the project we are collaborating on?
indian_law_reference: Indian Contract Act, 1872 - Section 23 (lawful object)

description: Clauses that assign all IP created during the employment period, without regard to whether it was created using company resources or time.
why_risky: This can lead to the employer claiming ownership over personal projects developed outside of work hours and resources.
what_to_look_for: Clauses that do not distinguish between work-related and personal IP.
safer_alternative: Limit IP assignment to work created using company resources or during work hours.
negotiation_leverage: Argue the importance of maintaining ownership of personal innovations for career growth.
negotiation_script: I propose we limit the IP assignment to work created using company resources or during work hours.
indian_law_reference: Indian Contract Act, 1872 - Section 10 (free consent)

description: Clauses that require freelancers to assign all IP rights to the client, including IP not directly related to the contracted work.
why_risky: Freelancers may lose rights to IP that could be used in future projects or for other clients, limiting their business potential.
what_to_look_for: Clauses that do not specify the IP is related to the work performed for the client.
safer_alternative: Restrict IP assignment to IP directly related to the deliverables of the contract.
negotiation_leverage: Point out the need to retain IP for future work and client engagements.
negotiation_script: Could we adjust the IP assignment to cover only the IP directly related to the deliverables of this contract?
indian_law_reference: Indian Contract Act, 1872 - Section 19 (voidability of agreements without free consent)

description: The contract specifies a foreign jurisdiction for dispute resolution, which may be costly and inconvenient for Indian parties.
why_risky: Foreign jurisdictions can lead to increased legal costs, unfamiliar legal procedures, and potential bias against Indian parties.
what_to_look_for: Check if the jurisdiction specified is outside India and if it is mandatory.
safer_alternative: Specify Indian jurisdiction or allow for mutual agreement on jurisdiction.
negotiation_leverage: Highlight the increased costs and complexities of foreign legal systems.
negotiation_script: I would prefer to have disputes resolved under Indian jurisdiction to minimize costs and ensure familiarity with the legal process.
indian_law_reference: Section 28 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872

description: The contract mandates arbitration in a foreign venue, which can be expensive and logistically challenging.
why_risky: Arbitration in a foreign venue can lead to high travel costs, unfamiliar arbitration rules, and potential bias.
what_to_look_for: Identify if the arbitration venue is outside India and if participation is mandatory.
safer_alternative: Propose arbitration in India or a neutral location agreed by both parties.
negotiation_leverage: Emphasize the financial and logistical burden of foreign arbitration.
negotiation_script: I propose we consider arbitration within India to reduce costs and logistical challenges.
indian_law_reference: Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996

description: The contract grants exclusive jurisdiction to foreign courts, potentially disadvantaging Indian parties.
why_risky: Exclusive foreign court jurisdiction can lead to increased legal fees, travel expenses, and unfamiliar legal systems.
what_to_look_for: Verify if the contract specifies exclusive jurisdiction of foreign courts.
safer_alternative: Negotiate for concurrent jurisdiction or Indian courts.
negotiation_leverage: Point out the potential for increased costs and legal complexities.
negotiation_script: I suggest we include an option for Indian courts to ensure fairness and manage costs.
indian_law_reference: Section 13 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908

description: The contract includes a unilateral arbitration clause favoring one party, often the foreign entity.
why_risky: Unilateral clauses can lead to biased arbitration outcomes and limit the legal recourse of the disadvantaged party.
what_to_look_for: Identify clauses that allow only one party to initiate arbitration.
safer_alternative: Propose mutual arbitration rights for both parties.
negotiation_leverage: Stress the importance of fairness and equal rights in dispute resolution.
negotiation_script: I recommend we revise the arbitration clause to allow both parties equal rights to initiate proceedings.
indian_law_reference: Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996

description: The contract contains vague or ambiguous terms regarding dispute resolution, leading to uncertainty.
why_risky: Ambiguity can result in prolonged disputes, increased legal costs, and unpredictable outcomes.
what_to_look_for: Check for lack of specificity in dispute resolution procedures.
safer_alternative: Define clear and specific dispute resolution procedures and timelines.
negotiation_leverage: Highlight the need for clarity to prevent future conflicts.
negotiation_script: To avoid potential disputes, let's specify a clear and detailed resolution process.
indian_law_reference: Indian Contract Act, 1872

description: A clause that mandates dispute resolution in a jurisdiction that is inconvenient or unfavorable to one party.
why_risky: It may lead to increased legal costs and logistical challenges, making it difficult for one party to pursue legal action.
red_flag_phrases: governed by the laws of [foreign location]
what_to_look_for: Check if the jurisdiction is outside of India or in a location that is logistically challenging.
safer_alternative: Specify a neutral or mutually convenient jurisdiction within India.
negotiation_leverage: Highlight the increased costs and inconvenience of litigating in a foreign jurisdiction.
negotiation_script: I would prefer if we could agree on a jurisdiction within India for dispute resolution to ensure fairness and manageability for both parties.
indian_law_reference: Section 20 of the Civil Procedure Code, 1908

description: A clause that requires arbitration to be conducted in a foreign location, potentially increasing costs and complexity.
why_risky: Arbitration in a foreign location can be prohibitively expensive and may not be governed by Indian arbitration laws.
what_to_look_for: Identify if the arbitration location is outside India and if foreign laws apply.
safer_alternative: Propose arbitration in a major Indian city under Indian arbitration laws.
negotiation_leverage: Emphasize the benefits of cost-effective and familiar legal processes within India.
negotiation_script: Given the potential costs and complexities, I suggest we conduct arbitration in India under Indian laws to ensure a fair process.
indian_law_reference: Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996

description: A clause that specifies the contract will be governed by the laws of a foreign country.
why_risky: Foreign governing laws can be unfamiliar and disadvantageous, potentially leading to unfavorable interpretations.
red_flag_phrases: governed by the laws of [foreign country]
what_to_look_for: Ensure the governing law is not from a foreign jurisdiction that could be biased.
safer_alternative: Opt for Indian laws to govern the contract to ensure familiarity and fairness.
negotiation_leverage: Point out the potential for misinterpretation and increased legal costs under foreign laws.
negotiation_script: To avoid potential legal complexities, I propose we use Indian law as the governing law for this contract.
indian_law_reference: Indian Contract Act, 1872

description: A clause that allows only one party to initiate arbitration, limiting the other party's options for dispute resolution.
why_risky: It creates an imbalance in dispute resolution rights, potentially leaving one party without recourse.
what_to_look_for: Ensure both parties have equal rights to initiate arbitration.
safer_alternative: Include a reciprocal arbitration clause allowing both parties equal rights.
negotiation_leverage: Argue for fairness and equality in dispute resolution mechanisms.
negotiation_script: For fairness, I propose we amend the clause to allow both parties the right to initiate arbitration.
indian_law_reference: Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996

description: A clause that defines Force Majeure events too broadly, potentially including foreseeable events.
why_risky: It allows one party to escape liability for non-performance due to events that should have been anticipated and mitigated.
what_to_look_for: Check if the clause lists specific events and excludes foreseeable risks.
safer_alternative: Limit the clause to truly unforeseeable and uncontrollable events.
negotiation_leverage: Highlight the need for predictability and fairness in contract obligations.
negotiation_script: I propose we refine the Force Majeure clause to specify only truly unforeseeable events to ensure fairness.
indian_law_reference: Indian Contract Act, 1872 - Section 56

description: A clause allowing one party to unilaterally declare a Force Majeure event without mutual agreement.
why_risky: It can be abused to delay or avoid obligations without proper justification.
what_to_look_for: Ensure mutual agreement is required to invoke Force Majeure.
safer_alternative: Require both parties to agree on the occurrence of a Force Majeure event.
negotiation_leverage: Emphasize the need for mutual consent to prevent misuse.
negotiation_script: To prevent potential misuse, let's require mutual agreement to invoke Force Majeure.
indian_law_reference: Indian Contract Act, 1872 - Section 32

description: A Force Majeure clause that does not require timely notice to the other party.
why_risky: Without a notice requirement, one party may claim Force Majeure retroactively, causing unexpected disruptions.
what_to_look_for: Ensure the clause includes a requirement for timely notice.
safer_alternative: Include a specific timeframe for providing notice of a Force Majeure event.
negotiation_leverage: Argue for clarity and predictability in contract performance.
negotiation_script: To ensure clarity, let's include a notice requirement for invoking Force Majeure.
indian_law_reference: Indian Contract Act, 1872 - Section 56

description: A clause that allows for an indefinite suspension of obligations due to Force Majeure.
why_risky: It can lead to prolonged uncertainty and potential financial loss.
what_to_look_for: Check for a maximum duration for Force Majeure suspension.
safer_alternative: Specify a reasonable maximum duration for suspension of obligations.
negotiation_leverage: Stress the importance of having a clear timeline to manage risks.
negotiation_script: To manage risks effectively, let's agree on a maximum duration for Force Majeure suspension.
indian_law_reference: Indian Contract Act, 1872 - Section 56

description: A clause that includes economic hardship as a Force Majeure event.
why_risky: Economic hardship is typically foreseeable and should not excuse non-performance.
what_to_look_for: Ensure economic conditions are excluded from Force Majeure events.
safer_alternative: Exclude economic hardship from the definition of Force Majeure.
negotiation_leverage: Point out that economic conditions are part of normal business risks.
negotiation_script: Economic conditions are part of normal business risks and should not be included as Force Majeure.
indian_law_reference: Indian Contract Act, 1872 - Section 56

description: A force majeure clause that is overly broad and includes events that are not typically considered unforeseeable.
why_risky: Such clauses can be abused to excuse non-performance for events that should be manageable, leading to unjustified contract termination or delay.
what_to_look_for: Check if the clause lists events that are typically within the control of the parties.
safer_alternative: Limit the clause to truly unforeseeable events like natural disasters.
negotiation_leverage: Highlight the need for predictability and fairness in contract performance.
negotiation_script: I propose we narrow the force majeure clause to include only truly unforeseeable events to ensure fairness.
indian_law_reference: Indian Contract Act, 1872 - Section 56

description: Force majeure clauses that do not specify clear notification requirements for invoking the clause.
why_risky: Lack of clarity can lead to disputes over whether the clause was properly invoked, causing delays and legal costs.
what_to_look_for: Ensure the clause specifies a clear timeframe and method for notification.
safer_alternative: Include specific timeframes and methods for notification, such as email or registered mail.
negotiation_leverage: Emphasize the importance of clear communication to avoid misunderstandings.
negotiation_script: To prevent disputes, let's specify a clear notification process for invoking force majeure.
indian_law_reference: Indian Contract Act, 1872 - Section 32

description: Force majeure clauses that allow for indefinite suspension of obligations.
why_risky: Indefinite suspension can lead to prolonged non-performance, affecting project timelines and financial stability.
what_to_look_for: Check if the clause specifies a maximum duration for suspension.
safer_alternative: Include a maximum duration for suspension, after which parties must renegotiate terms.
negotiation_leverage: Point out the need for certainty in project timelines and financial planning.
negotiation_script: To ensure project continuity, let's agree on a maximum suspension period for force majeure events.
indian_law_reference: Indian Contract Act, 1872 - Section 56

description: Clauses that do not clearly define when and how obligations should resume after a force majeure event.
why_risky: Ambiguity can lead to disputes over the timing and conditions for resuming obligations, causing operational disruptions.
what_to_look_for: Ensure the clause specifies clear criteria for resuming obligations.
safer_alternative: Define specific conditions and timelines for resumption of obligations.
negotiation_leverage: Stress the importance of operational continuity and clear expectations.
negotiation_script: To avoid operational disruptions, let's clarify the conditions for resuming obligations post-force majeure.
indian_law_reference: Indian Contract Act, 1872 - Section 56

description: Contracts with vague or unclear terms regarding the automatic renewal of services.
why_risky: Consumers may be unaware of the renewal, leading to unexpected charges.
what_to_look_for: Check for clear language on renewal terms and cancellation procedures.
safer_alternative: Explicitly state renewal terms and provide clear cancellation instructions.
negotiation_leverage: Highlight the need for transparency and consumer protection.
negotiation_script: I would like the renewal terms to be explicitly stated with clear cancellation procedures to avoid any misunderstandings.
indian_law_reference: Consumer Protection Act, 2019

description: Contracts that do not require notifying the consumer before renewal.
why_risky: Consumers may not be aware of the renewal date, leading to unwanted charges.
what_to_look_for: Ensure the contract includes a clause for prior notification before renewal.
safer_alternative: Include a mandatory notification period before renewal.
negotiation_leverage: Emphasize the importance of consumer awareness and informed consent.
negotiation_script: I request that a notification clause be added to inform me before any renewal takes place.
indian_law_reference: Consumer Protection Act, 2019

description: Contracts that lock consumers into long renewal periods without flexibility.
why_risky: Consumers may be trapped in a service they no longer need or want.
what_to_look_for: Look for clauses that specify long renewal periods and lack of exit options.
safer_alternative: Offer shorter renewal periods or flexible cancellation options.
negotiation_leverage: Argue for consumer flexibility and fair contract terms.
negotiation_script: I would prefer a shorter renewal period or the option to cancel without penalty.
indian_law_reference: Contract Act, 1872

description: Contracts that do not clearly disclose fees associated with renewal.
why_risky: Consumers may incur unexpected costs due to undisclosed fees.
what_to_look_for: Ensure all fees related to renewal are clearly disclosed in the contract.
safer_alternative: Provide a detailed breakdown of all potential renewal fees.
negotiation_leverage: Insist on transparency and full disclosure of all costs.
negotiation_script: I need a clear breakdown of all renewal fees to ensure there are no hidden costs.
indian_law_reference: Consumer Protection Act, 2019

description: Contracts that automatically renew without explicit consent from the consumer.
why_risky: Consumers may be bound to a contract they did not agree to renew.
what_to_look_for: Check for clauses that assume consent for renewal without explicit agreement.
safer_alternative: Require explicit consent from the consumer for each renewal.
negotiation_leverage: Stress the importance of consumer rights and explicit consent.
negotiation_script: I require that my explicit consent be obtained before any renewal is processed.
indian_law_reference: Consumer Protection Act, 2019

description: Contracts that automatically renew without clear terms or notice periods.
why_risky: Consumers may be unaware of renewal, leading to unexpected charges.
what_to_look_for: Check for clear renewal terms and notice requirements.
safer_alternative: Include explicit notice periods and opt-out options.
negotiation_leverage: Highlight potential consumer protection violations.
negotiation_script: I would like to discuss the renewal terms to ensure they are clear and fair, with adequate notice periods.
indian_law_reference: Consumer Protection Act, 2019

description: Contracts that renew automatically without an option to opt-out.
why_risky: Users may be locked into unwanted services, incurring additional costs.
what_to_look_for: Ensure there is a clear opt-out or cancellation clause.
safer_alternative: Provide a straightforward opt-out mechanism before renewal.
negotiation_leverage: Emphasize the importance of consumer choice and flexibility.
negotiation_script: I would like to request an opt-out clause to ensure flexibility and control over the renewal process.
indian_law_reference: Indian Contract Act, 1872

description: Contracts that include undisclosed fees upon automatic renewal.
why_risky: Unexpected financial burden due to hidden costs.
what_to_look_for: Review the payment terms for any mention of additional fees.
safer_alternative: Ensure all fees are clearly disclosed upfront.
negotiation_leverage: Point out the need for transparency in financial obligations.
negotiation_script: Could we clarify the renewal fees to ensure there are no hidden charges?
indian_law_reference: Consumer Protection Act, 2019

description: Contracts that renew automatically with insufficient notice to the user.
why_risky: Users may not have enough time to cancel, leading to unwanted renewals.
what_to_look_for: Check the notice period provided before renewal.
safer_alternative: Implement a reasonable notice period, such as 30 days.
negotiation_leverage: Argue for fair notice periods to allow informed decisions.
negotiation_script: I would appreciate a longer notice period before renewal to make an informed decision.
indian_law_reference: Indian Contract Act, 1872

description: Clauses that allow employers to excessively monitor employee data and communications.
why_risky: Such clauses can infringe on personal privacy and may lead to unauthorized data collection.
what_to_look_for: Look for clauses that specify the extent and method of monitoring.
safer_alternative: Limit monitoring to work-related activities with prior notice.
negotiation_leverage: Highlight the importance of privacy and the potential for legal challenges.
negotiation_script: I am concerned about the extent of data monitoring. Can we limit this to work-related activities only?
indian_law_reference: Information Technology Act, 2000

description: Clauses that permit sharing of personal data with third parties without explicit consent.
why_risky: This can lead to unauthorized data dissemination and potential breaches of privacy.
what_to_look_for: Check for any mention of data sharing with external entities.
safer_alternative: Require explicit consent for any third-party data sharing.
negotiation_leverage: Emphasize the need for data protection and user consent.
negotiation_script: I would like to ensure that my data is not shared with third parties without my explicit consent.
indian_law_reference: Personal Data Protection Bill, 2019

description: Clauses that do not specify how long personal data will be retained.
why_risky: Undefined retention periods can lead to indefinite storage of personal data, increasing the risk of misuse.
what_to_look_for: Look for specific time frames for data retention.
safer_alternative: Specify clear retention periods and conditions for data deletion.
negotiation_leverage: Point out the importance of data minimization and legal compliance.
negotiation_script: Can we include a specific data retention period to ensure compliance with data protection standards?
indian_law_reference: Information Technology (Reasonable Security Practices and Procedures and Sensitive Personal Data or Information) Rules, 2011

description: Clauses that are unclear about how collected data will be used.
why_risky: Vague terms can lead to misuse of data for purposes not originally intended or agreed upon.
what_to_look_for: Ensure that the purpose of data collection and usage is clearly defined.
safer_alternative: Specify the exact purposes for which data will be used.
negotiation_leverage: Stress the need for transparency and specific data usage terms.
negotiation_script: I would like to clarify the specific purposes for which my data will be used.
indian_law_reference: Personal Data Protection Bill, 2019

description: Clauses that do not specify adequate data protection measures.
why_risky: Lack of specific protection measures can lead to data breaches and unauthorized access.
what_to_look_for: Check for detailed descriptions of security measures and protocols.
safer_alternative: Include specific security protocols and compliance with recognized standards.
negotiation_leverage: Highlight the potential risks and legal liabilities of inadequate protection.
negotiation_script: Can we include specific data protection measures to ensure compliance with industry standards?
indian_law_reference: Information Technology Act, 2000

description: Clauses that allow employers to collect excessive personal data without clear limitations.
why_risky: Such clauses can lead to privacy violations and misuse of personal data, potentially breaching Indian data protection laws.
what_to_look_for: Check for clauses that do not specify the type of data collected or the purpose of collection.
safer_alternative: Limit data collection to what is necessary for the job and specify the purpose.
negotiation_leverage: Highlight the importance of data privacy and compliance with Indian laws.
negotiation_script: I am concerned about the broad data collection terms. Could we limit data collection to only what's necessary for my role?
indian_law_reference: Information Technology Act, 2000 and its amendments

description: Clauses that permit unrestricted monitoring of employees' or freelancers' activities.
why_risky: Unrestricted monitoring can infringe on personal privacy and may not comply with Indian privacy standards.
what_to_look_for: Identify clauses that allow for monitoring without specifying limits or purposes.
safer_alternative: Define specific conditions and purposes for monitoring activities.
negotiation_leverage: Emphasize the need for privacy and adherence to legal standards.
negotiation_script: The monitoring terms seem quite broad. Could we specify the scope and purpose of monitoring?
indian_law_reference: Right to Privacy under Article 21 of the Indian Constitution

description: Clauses that allow for ambiguous use of collected data.
why_risky: Ambiguity in data usage can lead to misuse and potential legal challenges under Indian law.
red_flag_phrases: data may be used for any purpose
what_to_look_for: Look for clauses that do not clearly define how data will be used.
safer_alternative: Specify the exact purposes for which data will be used.
negotiation_leverage: Point out the need for clarity to avoid future disputes.
negotiation_script: Could we clarify the specific purposes for which my data will be used?
indian_law_reference: Personal Data Protection Bill, 2019

description: Clauses that do not specify adequate data protection measures.
why_risky: Lack of specified data protection measures can lead to data breaches and non-compliance with Indian data protection laws.
what_to_look_for: Ensure clauses specify the security measures in place to protect data.
safer_alternative: Include detailed descriptions of data protection measures.
negotiation_leverage: Stress the importance of data security for both parties.
negotiation_script: I would like to ensure that there are specific data protection measures in place. Could we include more details?
indian_law_reference: Information Technology (Reasonable Security Practices and Procedures and Sensitive Personal Data or Information) Rules, 2011

description: A clause that broadly restricts the employee from soliciting any employees or clients of the employer.
why_risky: Such clauses can be overly broad and may not be enforceable under Indian law, which requires reasonable restrictions.
what_to_look_for: Check if the clause specifies a reasonable time frame and geographic scope.
safer_alternative: Limit the clause to key employees and clients with a specific time frame.
negotiation_leverage: Highlight the need for reasonable restrictions under Indian law.
negotiation_script: I understand the need for non-solicitation, but could we specify a reasonable time frame and limit it to key employees?
indian_law_reference: Section 27 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872

description: A clause that imposes a non-solicitation obligation for an indefinite period.
why_risky: Indefinite restrictions are generally considered unreasonable and unenforceable in India.
what_to_look_for: Ensure the clause has a clear and reasonable time limit.
safer_alternative: Specify a time limit, such as 6 months to 1 year.
negotiation_leverage: Argue that indefinite clauses are not enforceable and suggest a reasonable time frame.
negotiation_script: Could we agree on a specific time period for this clause to ensure it aligns with enforceable standards?
indian_law_reference: Section 27 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872

description: A clause that prohibits the solicitation of clients who are no longer associated with the employer.
why_risky: Restricting contact with former clients may be seen as unreasonable, especially if the relationship has ended.
what_to_look_for: Determine if the clause applies to current or former clients.
safer_alternative: Limit the restriction to current clients during the term of the contract.
negotiation_leverage: Point out the impracticality of restricting former client relationships.
negotiation_script: Could we adjust this clause to apply only to current clients during the contract term?
indian_law_reference: Section 27 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872

description: A clause that imposes a financial penalty for breaching non-solicitation terms.
why_risky: Penalties may be considered punitive and unenforceable under Indian law, which prefers compensatory damages.
what_to_look_for: Check if the clause specifies a penalty amount and its justification.
safer_alternative: Replace penalties with a clause for reasonable compensatory damages.
negotiation_leverage: Emphasize the legal preference for compensatory over punitive damages.
negotiation_script: I believe a compensatory damages approach would be more aligned with legal standards. Can we discuss this?
indian_law_reference: Section 74 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872

description: A clause that restricts solicitation of any business contacts made during employment.
why_risky: Such broad restrictions may be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable, as they can hinder professional growth.
what_to_look_for: Identify if the clause is overly broad and lacks specificity.
safer_alternative: Limit the restriction to key business contacts with a direct impact on the employer.
negotiation_leverage: Argue for the need to maintain professional networks for career development.
negotiation_script: Could we narrow this clause to focus on key contacts directly related to my role?
