indian_law_reference: Section 27 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872

description: A clause that broadly restricts an employee from soliciting any employees or clients of the company post-termination.
why_risky: Such clauses can be overly broad and may not be enforceable under Indian law, which requires restrictions to be reasonable in scope and duration.
what_to_look_for: Check if the clause specifies a reasonable time frame and is limited to key employees or clients.
safer_alternative: Limit the clause to specific key employees or clients and a reasonable time frame, such as 6 months.
negotiation_leverage: Highlight the need for the clause to be reasonable and specific to be enforceable.
negotiation_script: I understand the need for a non-solicitation clause, but could we specify it to key employees and a reasonable time frame to ensure enforceability?
indian_law_reference: Section 27 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872

description: A non-solicitation clause that does not specify an end date, effectively making it perpetual.
why_risky: Indian courts are unlikely to enforce perpetual restrictions as they are considered unreasonable and against public policy.
what_to_look_for: Ensure the clause specifies a clear and reasonable duration.
safer_alternative: Propose a specific duration, such as 12 months, which is more likely to be considered reasonable.
negotiation_leverage: Argue that a perpetual clause is unenforceable and propose a reasonable time limit.
negotiation_script: Could we agree on a specific duration for this clause? A perpetual restriction might not hold up legally.
indian_law_reference: Section 27 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872

description: A clause that prevents freelancers from soliciting clients they previously worked with, even after the contract ends.
why_risky: Restricting freelancers from working with former clients can be seen as a restraint of trade, which is generally unenforceable unless justified.
what_to_look_for: Check if the clause is limited to a reasonable time frame and specific clients.
safer_alternative: Limit the restriction to a short period, such as 3 months, and specific key clients.
negotiation_leverage: Emphasize the need for the clause to be reasonable to avoid being seen as a restraint of trade.
negotiation_script: I propose we limit this clause to a short period and specific clients to ensure it is reasonable and enforceable.
indian_law_reference: Section 27 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872

description: A clause that prohibits not only direct solicitation but also indirect actions, such as through third parties.
why_risky: Such clauses can be overly broad and difficult to enforce, as they may cover actions beyond the control of the individual.
what_to_look_for: Ensure the clause is clear about what constitutes indirect actions and is reasonable in scope.
safer_alternative: Clarify the scope of indirect actions and ensure it is limited to actions within the individual's control.
negotiation_leverage: Point out the difficulty in enforcing indirect actions and propose a more precise definition.
negotiation_script: Could we clarify what constitutes indirect actions in this clause to ensure it is enforceable and reasonable?
indian_law_reference: Section 27 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872

pattern_name: SaaS Vendor Lock-In via Proprietary Data Format
description: A SaaS provider stores your business data in a proprietary format and either charges exorbitant fees for data export or provides no export functionality at all. When you try to switch vendors, your data is effectively held hostage.
why_risky: Your entire business history — customer data, financial records, project files — becomes inaccessible if you leave the platform. Migration costs can exceed the entire value of your original contract. This creates artificial dependency and removes your negotiating power at renewal time.
red_flag_phrases: data is stored in our proprietary format
data migration services available at additional cost
data will be deleted 30 days after termination
what_to_look_for: Check: (1) Does the contract guarantee data export in a standard format (CSV, JSON, SQL)? (2) Is there a fee for exporting your data? (3) How long do you have to export after termination? (4) Does the vendor own or have rights to your data?
negotiation_leverage: Under Section 43A of the IT Act 2000 and the DPDP Act 2023, your business data remains your property. Demand contractual guarantees for: (1) free data export in standard formats, (2) minimum 90-day data retention post-termination, (3) API access for ongoing data portability.
safer_alternative: Insist on a clause that guarantees: 'All Customer Data shall be exportable in industry-standard formats (CSV, JSON, XML) at no additional cost. Upon termination, Customer shall have 90 days to export all data. Vendor shall provide reasonable export assistance at no charge.'
negotiation_script: I'd like to add a data portability clause. Our business data needs to be exportable in standard formats at no extra cost. Can we add a provision guaranteeing 90-day data retention post-termination with free export in CSV/JSON?
indian_law_reference: Section 43A, Information Technology Act, 2000; Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023

pattern_name: Unilateral SaaS Price Escalation Without Cap
description: The vendor reserves the right to increase subscription prices at any time, with little or no advance notice, and without any cap on the percentage increase. The customer's only remedy is to cancel — but cancellation may trigger data loss or early termination penalties.
why_risky: A small business that has built its operations around a SaaS tool cannot easily switch when the vendor doubles the price. Combined with data lock-in and annual commitments, this creates a 'boiling frog' scenario where costs escalate far beyond the original budget.
red_flag_phrases: prices are subject to change at any time
revised pricing will apply upon renewal
Company reserves the right to modify fees
continued use constitutes acceptance of new pricing
what_to_look_for: Check: (1) Is there a cap on annual price increases (e.g., CPI + 5%)? (2) How much advance notice is required before a price change? (3) Can you opt out without penalty if prices increase beyond a threshold? (4) Does 'continued use = acceptance' apply?
negotiation_leverage: Under Section 23 of the Indian Contract Act, an agreement must have certain consideration. A unilateral right to change pricing without limits makes the consideration uncertain and potentially voidable. Push for price caps and opt-out rights.
safer_alternative: Add: 'Annual price increases shall not exceed [CPI + 3%] or [10%], whichever is lower. Customer shall receive 90 days written notice of any price change and may terminate without penalty within 30 days of receiving notice if the increase exceeds the cap.'
negotiation_script: The current pricing clause gives you unlimited ability to raise prices. For budget predictability, we need a cap — say CPI plus 3% annually — with 90 days' notice and a no-penalty exit option if the increase exceeds the cap. Can we add this?
indian_law_reference: Section 23, Indian Contract Act, 1872 (lawful consideration); Section 2(46), Consumer Protection Act, 2019 (unfair contracts)

pattern_name: Vendor SLA with No Penalty for Breach
description: The contract includes a Service Level Agreement (SLA) promising 99.9% uptime, fast response times, or other performance metrics — but there are no penalties, service credits, or remedies if the vendor fails to meet these commitments.
why_risky: An SLA without teeth is just a marketing promise. If the vendor's platform goes down for 3 days and your business loses revenue, you have no contractual recourse. The SLA becomes merely aspirational, not a binding obligation.
red_flag_phrases: SLA is provided on a best-effort basis
SLA credits are at the sole discretion of the provider
what_to_look_for: Check: (1) Does the SLA specify automatic service credits for downtime? (2) Is there a right to terminate if SLA is repeatedly breached? (3) Are 'scheduled maintenance' windows excluded from uptime calculations? (4) Who determines whether an SLA breach occurred?
negotiation_leverage: An SLA without remedies is not a binding obligation under Indian contract law — it lacks consideration. Push for automatic service credits (e.g., 10% of monthly fee per hour of unplanned downtime) and termination rights for chronic SLA failure.
safer_alternative: Add: 'For each hour of unplanned downtime below the 99.9% SLA, Customer shall receive a service credit of 10% of the monthly fee, up to 100% of that month's fee. If the SLA is breached in 3 consecutive months, Customer may terminate without penalty.'
negotiation_script: The SLA promises 99.9% uptime but has no remedies for breach. We need automatic service credits — say 10% of monthly fees per hour of unplanned downtime — and a termination right if the SLA is breached three months running. Can we add this?
indian_law_reference: Section 73, Indian Contract Act, 1872 (compensation for breach); general principles of contract performance

description: The contract imposes substantial fees to terminate early or to export your own data upon termination. These fees can include 'early termination charges,' 'data migration fees,' or 'account closure processing fees' that are disproportionate to any actual cost incurred by the vendor.
why_risky: Exit fees create a financial barrier to leaving, even when the service is poor. A startup paying ₹10,000/month might face a ₹2,00,000 'early exit fee' — effectively trapping them for the entire contract period regardless of service quality.
red_flag_phrases: remaining contract value becomes due immediately
what_to_look_for: Check: (1) Is there an early termination fee? How much? (2) Is it proportional to the remaining contract value? (3) Does it decrease over time (pro-rata)? (4) Is data export charged separately? (5) Can you exit without penalty if the vendor breaches SLA?
negotiation_leverage: Under Section 74 of the Indian Contract Act, penalty clauses must be reasonable and proportional to actual loss. Disproportionate exit fees are likely unenforceable. Courts consistently reduce penalties to actual damages suffered (ONGC v. Saw Pipes, SC).
safer_alternative: Replace with: 'Either party may terminate with 30 days written notice. Early termination fees, if any, shall be limited to one month's subscription fee and shall reduce pro-rata over the contract period. Data export shall be free of charge.'
negotiation_script: The exit fees seem disproportionate. Under Section 74 of the Indian Contract Act, penalties must be proportional to actual loss. Can we cap the termination fee at one month's subscription and ensure data export is free?
indian_law_reference: Section 74, Indian Contract Act, 1872; ONGC v. Saw Pipes Ltd (SC) — reasonable compensation only

description: A clause that allows the vendor to unilaterally modify the terms of service, pricing, features, or scope of work at any time, with 'continued use constituting acceptance' of the new terms. The customer has no right to reject specific changes without terminating entirely.
why_risky: The vendor can fundamentally change what you signed up for — remove features you rely on, add usage limits, change privacy terms, or shift liability — and your only option is to accept or leave. This makes the original contract meaningless.
red_flag_phrases: we may modify these terms at any time
updated terms will be posted on our website
by using the service you agree to the latest terms
what_to_look_for: Check: (1) Can the vendor change terms unilaterally? (2) Is there advance notice (how many days)? (3) Do you have the right to reject changes without penalty? (4) Are 'material changes' defined differently from minor updates? (5) Does 'continued use = acceptance' apply?
negotiation_leverage: Under Section 2(46) of the Consumer Protection Act 2019, contracts with unilateral modification rights can be classified as 'unfair contracts.' Under general contract law, mutual consent is required for any material change — unilateral modification undermines the basis of the agreement.
safer_alternative: Replace with: 'Material changes to these terms require 60 days written notice and explicit written consent from the Customer. If Customer does not consent, the original terms remain in effect for the remainder of the contract period. Non-material changes (e.g., bug fixes) take effect immediately.'
negotiation_script: The terms give you the right to change any part of this agreement at any time. Under Section 2(46) of the Consumer Protection Act, that makes this potentially an unfair contract. Can we require mutual consent for material changes, with 60 days' notice?
indian_law_reference: Section 2(46), Consumer Protection Act, 2019; Sections 13-14, Indian Contract Act, 1872 (consent and free consent)

description: The contract charges subscription fees but also includes a clause granting the vendor a perpetual, irrevocable license to use, reproduce, modify, and create derivative works from the customer's data, content, or business processes — even after the contract ends.
why_risky: You are paying for a service but also unknowingly granting the vendor permanent rights to your business data. The vendor can use your proprietary processes, customer insights, or business intelligence to train AI models, create competing products, or sell anonymized data to third parties.
red_flag_phrases: right to use, reproduce, modify, and create derivative works
anonymized and aggregated data may be used
what_to_look_for: Check: (1) Does the vendor claim any license to YOUR data? (2) Does the license 'survive termination'? (3) Can the vendor use your data to train AI models or create competing products? (4) Is 'anonymized data' use permitted — and who defines what counts as anonymized?
negotiation_leverage: Under Section 17 of the Indian Copyright Act, you are the first owner of your original works. Under the DPDP Act 2023, personal data processing requires specific consent. A perpetual license buried in a SaaS contract likely lacks informed consent and may be voidable.
safer_alternative: Replace with: 'Vendor is granted a limited, non-exclusive license to process Customer Data solely for the purpose of providing the subscribed services. This license terminates immediately upon contract termination. Vendor shall not use Customer Data for any other purpose including analytics, AI training, or product development.'
negotiation_script: This clause grants you a perpetual license to our business data even after we leave. That's far too broad. We need the license limited to service delivery only, terminating when the contract ends. Can we revise this?
indian_law_reference: Section 17, Indian Copyright Act, 1957; Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023

pattern_name: Broad Vendor Indemnification from Small Business
description: The customer must indemnify the vendor against all claims, losses, and damages 'arising out of or related to' the customer's use of the service — including claims caused by the vendor's own defects, security breaches, or compliance failures.
why_risky: If the vendor's platform gets hacked and your customer data leaks, YOU are indemnifying THEM. If the vendor's tool produces wrong outputs that cause a third-party claim, YOU pay. The small business bears the risk for the vendor's product quality.
red_flag_phrases: Customer shall indemnify, defend, and hold harmless the Provider
arising out of or related to Customer's use
including but not limited to third-party claims
what_to_look_for: Check: (1) Is indemnification mutual or one-sided? (2) Does the vendor indemnify YOU for IP infringement, data breaches, or product defects? (3) Is the scope limited to your actual negligence or overly broad ('arising out of use')? (4) Is there a cap on indemnification costs?
negotiation_leverage: Under Sections 124-125 of the Indian Contract Act, indemnification must be based on actual loss from the indemnifier's actions. An indemnity clause that makes you pay for the vendor's failures lacks legal basis. Courts require proportionality.
safer_alternative: Replace with mutual indemnification: 'Each party shall indemnify the other against claims arising from its own negligence, breach, or willful misconduct. Vendor specifically indemnifies Customer against: (a) IP infringement claims, (b) data breaches caused by Vendor's security failures, (c) regulatory non-compliance of Vendor's platform.'
negotiation_script: The indemnification is one-sided — we indemnify you but you don't indemnify us. We need mutual indemnification, and you should specifically indemnify us against data breaches and IP infringement claims arising from your platform. Can we balance this?
indian_law_reference: Sections 124-125, Indian Contract Act, 1872 (Contract of Indemnity)

description: A clause that requires the business to purchase exclusively from one supplier or vendor for the contract duration, preventing the business from sourcing alternatives even if better or cheaper options emerge.
why_risky: Exclusivity removes your leverage. If the supplier raises prices, delivers poor quality, or has supply chain issues, you are contractually barred from seeking alternatives. This is especially dangerous for startups that need agility.
red_flag_phrases: shall not procure from any other provider
what_to_look_for: Check: (1) Is exclusivity mutual (they also commit to you)? (2) What is the duration — exclusivity for over 1 year is aggressive for a startup. (3) Is there an exit clause if the supplier fails performance benchmarks? (4) Can you source alternatives if the supplier can't deliver?
negotiation_leverage: Exclusivity must benefit both parties. Under Section 27 of the Indian Contract Act, agreements in restraint of trade are void. While supplier exclusivity isn't strictly 'restraint of trade,' the principle of reasonableness applies. Push for performance-linked exclusivity.
safer_alternative: Add: 'Exclusivity is contingent on Supplier maintaining [quality/price/delivery] benchmarks as specified in Schedule A. If Supplier fails to meet benchmarks for two consecutive quarters, Customer may source from alternative suppliers without penalty.'
negotiation_script: We're open to preferred-supplier status but not absolute exclusivity. Can we make exclusivity conditional on you meeting quality and delivery benchmarks, with an exit option if benchmarks aren't met for two quarters?
indian_law_reference: Section 27, Indian Contract Act, 1872 (restraint of trade); general principles of reasonableness

pattern_name: Automatic Price Escalation Without CPI Cap
description: The contract includes an automatic annual price escalation clause (e.g., 15-20% per year) that is not linked to any index like CPI (Consumer Price Index) and has no maximum cap, compounding year over year.
why_risky: A 15% annual escalation compounds to a 75% increase over 4 years. If your contract started at ₹1,00,000/month, you'd be paying ₹1,75,000/month by year 4 — with no ability to negotiate. This significantly erodes margins for small businesses.
what_to_look_for: Check: (1) Is the escalation percentage fixed or linked to CPI? (2) Is there a maximum cap? (3) Does escalation compound? (4) Can you renegotiate at each renewal? (5) What happens if CPI is actually negative (deflation)?
negotiation_leverage: Standard commercial practice in India ties escalation to CPI (typically 4-6%). An arbitrary 15% escalation is more than double the RBI's inflation target of 4%. Push for CPI-linked escalation with a reasonable cap.
safer_alternative: Replace with: 'Annual price escalation shall be equal to the Consumer Price Index (CPI) change for the preceding 12 months, subject to a minimum of 0% and a maximum of 8%. Escalation shall apply only on the anniversary of the contract start date.'
negotiation_script: A 15% annual escalation far exceeds the RBI's inflation target of 4%. Can we link the escalation to CPI with a cap of 8%? That protects both of us — you against cost increases, and us against runaway pricing.
indian_law_reference: General contract law principles; RBI monetary policy framework (inflation targeting at 4%)

description: The contract makes your payment contingent on the client's own client paying them first ('pay-when-paid') or on a third party's approval that you have no visibility into or control over.
why_risky: You complete work and submit an invoice, but payment is held because the client's end-customer hasn't paid yet — something entirely outside your control. This can delay payments by months and shifts the client's credit risk onto your small business.
red_flag_phrases: payment upon receipt from end client
what_to_look_for: Check: (1) Is payment tied to any third party's action? (2) Is there a maximum wait period regardless of third-party payment? (3) What happens if the end client goes bankrupt? (4) Can you terminate and demand payment if payment is delayed beyond a threshold?
negotiation_leverage: Indian courts generally disfavor 'pay-when-paid' clauses. Your obligation is to the party who hired you, not to their client. Under Section 73 of the ICA, you are entitled to compensation for breach regardless of third-party issues.
safer_alternative: Replace with: 'Payment shall be due within 30 days of invoice submission, regardless of Client's own payment collections. If Client's end-customer delays payment, this shall not affect Vendor's right to timely payment under this agreement.'
negotiation_script: This 'pay-when-paid' clause transfers your client's credit risk to us. Our obligation is to deliver work to you, and your obligation is to pay us for it — regardless of your own collections. Can we set a firm 30-day payment term?
indian_law_reference: Section 73, Indian Contract Act, 1872; general principles of privity of contract

pattern_name: Unrestricted Audit Rights Without Notice or Limits
description: The vendor or franchisor reserves the right to audit your business operations, financial records, or use of the platform 'at any time, without prior notice.' There is no limit on frequency, scope, or who bears the cost of audits.
why_risky: Unrestricted audit rights can be used as a harassment tool. The vendor can demand access to your financial records, disrupt your operations, and bill you for audit costs — especially if they suspect you're considering switching vendors or renegotiating terms.
red_flag_phrases: audit at any time without notice
access to all books and records
audit costs borne by the audited party
what_to_look_for: Check: (1) How much advance notice is required? (Minimum 15 business days is standard.) (2) How many audits per year? (Maximum 1-2 is standard.) (3) Who pays audit costs if no issues are found? (4) Is the scope limited to the contract or does it extend to your entire business?
negotiation_leverage: Even tax authorities under the Income Tax Act give advance notice for audits. Unrestricted, no-notice audit rights are commercially unreasonable. Push for structured audit provisions with notice, frequency limits, and cost allocation.
safer_alternative: Replace with: 'Auditor may conduct a maximum of one audit per 12-month period, with a minimum of 15 business days written notice. Audit scope shall be limited to compliance with this agreement. Audit costs shall be borne by the auditing party, unless a material breach is discovered.'
negotiation_script: The audit clause gives you unlimited, no-notice access to our records. That's operationally disruptive. Can we limit it to one audit per year with 15 business days' notice, with costs borne by the auditing party unless a breach is found?
indian_law_reference: General principles of reasonable commercial practice; Right to Privacy under Article 21 (as applied to business entities)

pattern_name: Overbroad Survival Clauses That Outlive the Contract
description: A 'survival' clause that states broad sections of the agreement — including non-compete, exclusivity, pricing restrictions, and indemnification — continue to bind you even after the contract has terminated, sometimes indefinitely.
why_risky: While it's normal for NDA and IP provisions to survive termination, having non-compete, exclusivity, pricing obligations, and unlimited indemnification survive indefinitely turns a time-limited contract into a permanent obligation.
red_flag_phrases: the following provisions shall survive termination indefinitely
shall continue in full force and effect after termination
obligations under Sections X through Y shall survive
what_to_look_for: Check: (1) Which specific sections survive? (NDA and IP are normal; non-compete and pricing restrictions are not.) (2) Is there a time limit on survival? (2-3 years is standard for NDAs.) (3) Do indemnification obligations survive indefinitely? (They shouldn't.)
negotiation_leverage: Survival clauses must be reasonable in scope and duration. Perpetual survival of commercial obligations has no legal justification under the Indian Contract Act. Only obligations related to pre-termination breaches and genuine trade secrets should survive.
safer_alternative: Replace with: 'The following provisions shall survive termination for a period of 2 years: Confidentiality (Section X), Indemnification (Section Y, limited to claims arising prior to termination), and IP Ownership (Section Z). All other provisions shall terminate upon contract termination.'
negotiation_script: The survival clause is too broad — it keeps non-compete and pricing obligations alive indefinitely. Can we limit survival to confidentiality and IP for 2 years, and indemnification only for pre-termination claims?
indian_law_reference: General principles of Indian Contract Act, 1872; Section 27 (restraint of trade post-termination is void)

description: The supplier requires you to give them 'most favored customer' terms — meaning you cannot offer better pricing, terms, or service levels to any other supplier or partner — but the supplier does not reciprocate by guaranteeing you their best pricing.
why_risky: An MFN clause that only binds you restricts your ability to negotiate better deals with other vendors and partners. It effectively fixes your commercial terms at the lowest common denominator, harming your competitive positioning.
red_flag_phrases: shall not offer more favorable terms to any third party
what_to_look_for: Check: (1) Is the MFN clause mutual (both parties guarantee best terms)? (2) Does it apply to pricing only or all terms? (3) Is there a duration limit? (4) How is 'most favorable' defined — does it account for volume, payment terms, and scope differences?
negotiation_leverage: One-sided MFN clauses can raise antitrust concerns under the Competition Act, 2002. They can also constitute an unreasonable restraint on your business flexibility. Push for reciprocity or narrower scope.
safer_alternative: Either make it mutual ('Both parties agree to offer each other terms no less favorable than those offered to similarly situated customers/suppliers') or remove it entirely and negotiate pricing on merit for each renewal period.
negotiation_script: The MFN clause only binds us but not you. If we're guaranteeing you our best terms, we need the same guarantee from you. Can we either make this mutual or remove it and negotiate pricing on merit?
indian_law_reference: Competition Act, 2002 (anti-competitive agreements); general contract principles of mutuality

pattern_name: Total Warranty Disclaimer with No Remedy
description: The vendor disclaims ALL warranties — express, implied, statutory — and provides the product/service 'AS IS' with no guarantee of fitness for purpose, merchantability, or accuracy. There is no warranty period, no defect liability, and no remedy if the product fails.
why_risky: If the software crashes, the hardware fails, or the service doesn't work as advertised, you have no contractual remedy. The vendor can deliver a defective product and claim they never promised it would work.
red_flag_phrases: no guarantee of merchantability or fitness
what_to_look_for: Check: (1) Does the vendor provide ANY warranty? (2) Is there a defect liability period? (3) What remedy do you have if the product is defective? (4) Does the vendor at least warrant that the product does what its documentation says?
negotiation_leverage: Under Section 16 of the Sale of Goods Act, 1930, there are implied warranties of fitness and merchantability that cannot be entirely disclaimed when the buyer relies on the seller's skill and judgment. Complete warranty disclaimers may not be enforceable.
safer_alternative: Replace with: 'Vendor warrants that the Service shall: (a) materially conform to its published documentation, (b) be free from material defects for 12 months, and (c) comply with applicable Indian laws. Vendor's remedy for breach of warranty shall be, at Customer's option, repair, replacement, or refund.'
negotiation_script: A complete warranty disclaimer means we have no recourse if the product doesn't work. Under the Sale of Goods Act, implied warranties apply. Can we add a basic warranty that the product conforms to its documentation, with a repair-or-refund remedy?
indian_law_reference: Section 16, Sale of Goods Act, 1930 (implied conditions as to quality); Section 2(46), Consumer Protection Act, 2019

pattern_name: Change Order Pricing at Vendor's Sole Discretion
description: The contract allows the vendor to price change orders, scope additions, or out-of-scope work 'at their standard rates' or 'at their sole discretion,' without providing a rate card upfront or requiring customer approval before work begins.
why_risky: The vendor can claim any modification is 'out of scope,' perform the work without your approval, and then bill you at inflated rates. Without a pre-agreed rate card, you have no basis to dispute the charges.
red_flag_phrases: change orders will be billed at our standard rates
out-of-scope work at vendor's prevailing rates
what_to_look_for: Check: (1) Is there a pre-agreed rate card for change orders? (2) Must the vendor get written approval before starting out-of-scope work? (3) Is there a cap on change order costs relative to the original contract? (4) What constitutes 'in scope' vs 'out of scope'?
negotiation_leverage: Under general contract principles, both parties must agree on consideration before work is performed. Unilateral pricing violates the principle of consensus ad idem. Push for a pre-agreed rate card and mandatory written approval.
safer_alternative: Add: 'All change orders shall be priced according to the rate card in Schedule B. No out-of-scope work shall commence without a written Change Order signed by both parties, specifying scope, timeline, and cost. Unauthorized work shall not be billable.'
negotiation_script: We need a pre-agreed rate card for change orders, and no out-of-scope work should start without our written approval. Can we add a change order process that requires a signed scope document before work begins?
indian_law_reference: Sections 10 and 23, Indian Contract Act, 1872 (lawful consideration must be certain)

description: A loan agreement that imposes a steep penalty (2-5% of outstanding principal) for prepaying or foreclosing the loan before the tenure ends. The penalty is often buried in fine print and not disclosed during the sales pitch.
why_risky: If you get a bonus or inheritance and want to pay off your home or car loan early, you could lose ₹50,000-₹5,00,000 in prepayment penalties. The bank profits from keeping you in debt for the full tenure, and the penalty discourages early freedom from debt.
what_to_look_for: Check: (1) Is there a prepayment penalty? What percentage? (2) Does it apply to part-prepayment or only full foreclosure? (3) Is there a lock-in period before prepayment is allowed? (4) RBI has banned prepayment charges on floating rate home loans — does this apply?
negotiation_leverage: RBI has prohibited prepayment penalties on floating-rate home loans (RBI circular DBOD.Dir.BC.No.8/13.03.00/2012-13). For other loan types, prepayment charges exceeding 2% are considered excessive. Ask the lender to match RBI guidelines.
safer_alternative: For home loans: No prepayment penalty on floating rate loans (RBI mandated). For other loans: Prepayment penalty of maximum 1% of outstanding, reducing to 0% after 2 years. Part-prepayment of up to 25% of outstanding annually without any penalty.
negotiation_script: RBI has banned prepayment penalties on floating-rate home loans. Even for fixed-rate loans, the charges here seem high. Can we cap the prepayment penalty at 1% and allow 25% part-prepayment annually without charges?
indian_law_reference: RBI Circular DBOD.Dir.BC.No.8/13.03.00/2012-13; RBI Fair Practices Code for Lenders

description: A loan marketed as a 'low floating rate' that includes a clause allowing the lender to convert it to a fixed rate at their discretion, or vice versa, often during unfavorable rate environments. The switch trigger is vaguely defined.
why_risky: You take a floating-rate loan at 8.5% expecting it to drop with RBI rate cuts. Instead, the lender switches it to a fixed rate of 10.5% just before rates drop, locking you into a higher rate. The conversion clause is deeply buried and the trigger conditions are at the 'lender's discretion.'
red_flag_phrases: lender may convert the rate structure
rate type may be changed at the discretion of the bank
applicable rate will be determined by the lender
what_to_look_for: Check: (1) Can the lender unilaterally change the rate type (floating to fixed)? (2) Under what conditions? (3) Is the benchmark rate (MCLR/EBLR/repo) clearly stated? (4) How often can rates be revised? (5) Are you notified before a rate change?
negotiation_leverage: RBI requires all new floating-rate loans to be linked to an external benchmark (repo rate) since October 2019. Unilateral rate conversion violates the RBI's Fair Practices Code. The lender must transparently disclose the benchmark and spread.
safer_alternative: Insist on: 'Loan interest rate shall be [External Benchmark Rate + fixed spread of X%]. The spread shall remain constant for the loan tenure. Any change in interest rate shall be solely due to changes in the external benchmark and shall be communicated 30 days in advance.'
negotiation_script: RBI mandates that floating-rate loans be linked to an external benchmark with a fixed spread. This clause allows unilateral rate changes beyond benchmark movements. Can we fix the spread and link rate changes solely to the RBI repo rate?
indian_law_reference: RBI Circular on External Benchmark Linked Lending (2019); RBI Fair Practices Code for Lenders

description: A rental agreement that allows the landlord to forfeit all or a significant portion of the security deposit for vaguely defined reasons like 'damage beyond normal wear and tear,' 'cleaning charges,' or 'restoration to original condition' — without providing itemized deductions or photographic evidence.
why_risky: Tenants routinely lose ₹50,000-₹3,00,000 in security deposits because landlords claim vague 'damages' without proof. Without clear refund procedures, the tenant has no practical way to dispute deductions except through slow court proceedings.
red_flag_phrases: deposit may be forfeited for damages
restoration to original condition at tenant's expense
what_to_look_for: Check: (1) Is the deposit amount within state limits? (Most states cap at 2-3 months' rent.) (2) Is there a clear refund timeline? (3) Must the landlord provide itemized deductions with evidence? (4) Is there a joint inspection at move-in and move-out? (5) Does the landlord pay interest on the deposit?
negotiation_leverage: Most state Rent Control Acts (e.g., Karnataka Rent Act, Maharashtra Rent Control Act) cap security deposits and mandate refund within 1-3 months of vacating. Arbitrary forfeiture violates these statutes. A joint inspection clause protects both parties.
safer_alternative: Add: 'Security deposit of [X months rent] shall be refunded within 30 days of vacating, after deducting only documented damages beyond normal wear and tear. A joint inspection shall be conducted at move-in and move-out, with photographic evidence. Landlord shall provide itemized deductions within 15 days.'
negotiation_script: The deposit clause allows deductions at your discretion without evidence. Can we add a joint inspection at move-in/out, require itemized deductions with photos, and set a 30-day refund timeline? This protects both of us.
indian_law_reference: State-specific Rent Control Acts; Model Tenancy Act, 2021 (Section 8 — deposit limits and refund)

description: An e-commerce platform or retailer that denies cash refunds entirely and only offers store credit or exchange, even for defective products, wrong items, or items not matching the description — in clear violation of consumer protection rights.
why_risky: Store credit traps your money on the platform. If you received a defective product worth ₹15,000 and are forced to accept store credit, you are essentially giving the platform an interest-free loan. You may never find a suitable replacement on that platform.
red_flag_phrases: refunds will be issued as store credit only
refund in the form of wallet balance
what_to_look_for: Check: (1) Does the policy allow cash refunds for defective or misrepresented products? (2) Is there a return window? (3) Who pays return shipping? (4) For digital goods — is there a cooling-off period? (5) Are 'final sale' items clearly labeled before purchase?
negotiation_leverage: Under Sections 2(6) and 84-87 of the Consumer Protection Act 2019, consumers have the right to seek refund, replacement, or compensation for defective goods or deficient services. A blanket no-refund policy does not override statutory consumer rights.
safer_alternative: Policy should state: 'Full refund within 7-15 days for defective products, wrong items, or items not matching the description. Return shipping costs borne by the seller for seller-fault returns. Store credit offered as an alternative but not the only option.'
negotiation_script: Under the Consumer Protection Act 2019, I have the right to a refund for defective or misrepresented goods. Your store-credit-only policy doesn't override this statutory right. I'd like a full refund to my original payment method.
indian_law_reference: Sections 2(6), 84-87, Consumer Protection Act, 2019; E-Commerce Rules, 2020

description: An insurance policy that advertises broad coverage but uses fine-print exclusions to deny the most common types of claims. For example, a health insurance policy that excludes pre-existing conditions for 4+ years, or a motor insurance that excludes 'wear and tear' on parts that commonly fail.
why_risky: You pay premiums for years believing you're covered, only to discover at claim time that the specific situation you need coverage for falls under an exclusion. The insurer profits from premiums while denying the claims you actually need.
what_to_look_for: Check: (1) Read the ENTIRE exclusions list, not just the coverage summary. (2) What is the waiting period for pre-existing conditions? (IRDAI caps this at 4 years for health insurance.) (3) Are common claims (e.g., diabetes, knee surgery, flood damage) excluded? (4) Are there 'sub-limits' that cap payouts far below the sum insured?
negotiation_leverage: IRDAI regulations mandate that exclusions must be clearly communicated at the point of sale. The Insurance Ombudsman can intervene if exclusions were not properly disclosed. Under Section 45 of the Insurance Act, after 3 years the insurer cannot challenge the policy on grounds of misstatement.
safer_alternative: Before purchasing: (1) Request the full policy wording (not just the brochure). (2) Ask for a list of ALL exclusions. (3) Specifically ask about coverage for your most likely claims. (4) Compare the sub-limits against actual hospital/repair costs. (5) Choose policies with shorter waiting periods (1-2 years max).
negotiation_script: I'd like to see the complete list of exclusions before purchasing. Can you also confirm the waiting period for pre-existing conditions and any sub-limits? I want to ensure the coverage I'm paying for is actually usable.
indian_law_reference: Section 45, Insurance Act, 1938; IRDAI (Protection of Policyholders' Interests) Regulations, 2017

description: A gym or club membership contract that locks you in for 12-36 months with no cancellation option, requires in-person cancellation during limited hours, auto-renews without consent, and charges cancellation fees equal to the remaining membership value.
why_risky: You sign a ₹50,000 annual gym membership, relocate for work after 3 months, and discover you owe the remaining ₹37,500 as a 'cancellation fee.' The gym designed the contract to make leaving as expensive as staying.
red_flag_phrases: no cancellation during the membership period
cancellation fee equal to remaining balance
cancellation must be done in person
what_to_look_for: Check: (1) Is there a cooling-off period (7-14 days)? (2) Can you cancel if you relocate or have a medical condition? (3) Is cancellation available online/email or only in-person? (4) What is the cancellation fee? (5) Does auto-renewal require separate consent?
negotiation_leverage: Under Section 2(46) of the Consumer Protection Act 2019, contracts that impose disproportionate penalties are 'unfair contracts.' A cancellation fee equal to the remaining balance is arguably disproportionate. Consumer commissions have ruled in favor of consumers in gym contract disputes.
safer_alternative: Insist on: 'Member may cancel with 30 days written notice (email accepted). A pro-rata refund shall be issued for unused months. Cancellation without penalty for medical reasons or relocation (with proof). Auto-renewal requires separate written consent 30 days before renewal date.'
negotiation_script: The cancellation terms are very restrictive. Under the Consumer Protection Act, disproportionate cancellation fees can be classified as unfair. Can we add a 30-day notice cancellation with a pro-rata refund for unused months, and require opt-in for auto-renewal?
indian_law_reference: Section 2(46) & 47, Consumer Protection Act, 2019 (unfair contracts)

description: A subscription service (streaming, cloud storage, software) that reserves the right to increase prices mid-subscription and treats continued use as acceptance. The subscriber may not even receive notification — the new price simply appears on the next bill.
why_risky: You budget ₹999/month for a streaming service. Without notice, it becomes ₹1,499/month. By the time you notice the increased charge on your credit card, you've already been billed at the higher rate for months with no recourse.
red_flag_phrases: prices may be revised at any time
updated pricing will be reflected in your next bill
we reserve the right to change subscription fees
what_to_look_for: Check: (1) Does the service commit to price stability for the subscription period? (2) How much advance notice is given for price changes? (3) Can you opt out without penalty when prices increase? (4) Is 'continued use = acceptance' the only mechanism?
negotiation_leverage: Under the Consumer Protection Act 2019, changing material terms (like pricing) without adequate notice and explicit consent can constitute an unfair trade practice. The E-Commerce Rules 2020 require explicit consent for charges.
safer_alternative: Service should: (1) Guarantee current price for the subscription period. (2) Provide 30 days advance email/SMS notice of price changes. (3) Allow cancellation without penalty within 15 days of price change notice. (4) Require active consent (click-to-accept) for the new price, not passive 'continued use.'
negotiation_script: Under E-Commerce Rules 2020, I need explicit notice before price changes and the option to cancel. Can you confirm that my current rate is locked in for the subscription period, and I'll be notified 30 days before any increase?
indian_law_reference: Consumer Protection Act, 2019; Consumer Protection (E-Commerce) Rules, 2020

pattern_name: Personal Guarantor Trap in Business Loans
description: A business loan that requires the founder or director to sign as a personal guarantor, making them personally liable for the entire outstanding amount — including their home, savings, and personal assets — if the business defaults.
why_risky: The entire point of incorporating a company is limited liability. A personal guarantee destroys this protection. If your startup fails (as most do), you don't just lose the business — you lose your home, savings, and personal financial security.
red_flag_phrases: guarantor's personal assets may be attached
what_to_look_for: Check: (1) Is the personal guarantee limited to a percentage of the loan or unlimited? (2) Does the guarantee continue after you leave the company? (3) Can the bank directly attach your personal assets without first pursuing the company? (4) Does the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code (IBC) Section 95 process apply to you as a personal guarantor?
negotiation_leverage: Under the IBC (as amended 2021), personal guarantors can now be brought under insolvency proceedings. However, you can negotiate: (1) cap the guarantee amount, (2) limit personal assets subject to the guarantee, (3) add a 'last resort' clause requiring the bank to exhaust company assets first.
safer_alternative: Negotiate: 'Personal guarantee shall be limited to [50% of outstanding or ₹X], whichever is lower. Bank shall first pursue all remedies against the Company (including company assets, receivables, and insurance proceeds) before invoking the personal guarantee. Guarantee shall automatically release upon the Guarantor ceasing to be a Director.'
negotiation_script: I understand the need for a personal guarantee for a startup loan, but I need to limit my exposure. Can we cap the guarantee at 50% of outstanding, require the bank to exhaust company assets first, and release the guarantee if I leave the company?
indian_law_reference: Sections 126-147, Indian Contract Act, 1872 (Guarantee); Section 95, Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code, 2016 (personal guarantors)

description: An e-commerce platform's terms of service that disclaim all liability for products sold by third-party sellers on their platform — including defective products, counterfeit goods, and misleading descriptions — stating they are 'merely a marketplace.'
why_risky: When you receive a counterfeit or dangerous product from an e-commerce platform, the platform claims it's the seller's fault and refuses to help. The seller may be untraceable. You're left with a defective product and no recourse.
red_flag_phrases: no liability for products sold by third-party sellers
platform is not responsible for seller claims
what_to_look_for: Check: (1) Does the platform take any responsibility for seller verification? (2) Is there a buyer protection program? (3) Can you escalate to the platform if the seller is unresponsive? (4) Does the platform facilitate refunds for defective or counterfeit goods?
negotiation_leverage: Under the Consumer Protection (E-Commerce) Rules 2020, e-commerce entities have obligations regarding seller verification, grievance redressal, and product liability. The platform cannot simply disclaim all liability while profiting from the transaction.
safer_alternative: Choose platforms that: (1) Verify seller identity and product authenticity. (2) Offer buyer protection programs with guaranteed refunds. (3) Provide responsive grievance redressal (within 48 hours as per E-Commerce Rules). (4) Accept product liability for fulfilled-by-platform items.
negotiation_script: Under the E-Commerce Rules 2020, the platform has grievance redressal obligations. The seller is unresponsive and the product is defective. I'd like to escalate this to your Grievance Officer and request a refund under your buyer protection program.
indian_law_reference: Consumer Protection (E-Commerce) Rules, 2020; Sections 84-87, Consumer Protection Act, 2019 (product liability)

description: Terms of service for digital content (e-books, movies, games, software) that state you are purchasing a 'license' not ownership, and the provider can revoke your access to purchased content at any time without refund — even for content you've already paid for.
why_risky: You buy ₹50,000 worth of e-books or a ₹5,000 software license, then the provider removes the content from their platform, goes bankrupt, or terminates your account for a TOS violation. You lose access to everything with no refund.
red_flag_phrases: you are purchasing a license, not ownership
content may be removed at any time
access is subject to continued availability
what_to_look_for: Check: (1) Are you buying ownership or a license? (2) Under what conditions can access be revoked? (3) Is there a refund for revoked content? (4) Can you download content for offline access? (5) What happens if the platform shuts down?
negotiation_leverage: Under the Consumer Protection Act 2019, a digital purchase creates an obligation to provide the promised service. Revoking access to paid content without cause or refund is a deficiency in service. File a complaint with the Consumer Forum.
safer_alternative: Look for services that: (1) Allow downloading for permanent offline access. (2) Promise that purchased (not rented) content will remain accessible. (3) Offer refunds if content is removed from the platform. (4) Provide DRM-free options where possible.
negotiation_script: I purchased this content, and revoking access without refund is a deficiency in service under the Consumer Protection Act. I'd like either continued access or a full refund for all content that has been made unavailable.
indian_law_reference: Sections 2(11), 35, Consumer Protection Act, 2019 (deficiency in service)

description: A contract that requires the consumer to explicitly waive their statutory cooling-off period (the 'free look' period in insurance, or cancellation window in other contracts), or makes the waiver a condition of availing a 'discount' or 'special offer.'
why_risky: Cooling-off periods exist specifically to protect consumers from high-pressure sales tactics. Waiving them means you cannot cancel even if you realize the product is unsuitable, was misrepresented, or you were pressured into buying.
red_flag_phrases: by availing this offer, you waive the free-look period
this special rate is contingent on waiving the cooling-off period
no refund during or after the free-look period
what_to_look_for: Check: (1) Is the cooling-off/free-look period clearly stated? (IRDAI mandates 15-30 days for insurance.) (2) Is it being waived as part of a 'deal'? (3) Can you exercise the cooling-off period through email/phone or only in person? (4) What is the refund process?
negotiation_leverage: IRDAI mandates a 15-day free-look period (30 days for online purchases) for ALL insurance policies — this cannot be contractually waived. Similarly, RBI guidelines provide cooling-off periods for certain financial products. Any waiver clause is void and unenforceable.
safer_alternative: The cooling-off period is a statutory right and cannot be waived. If a seller asks you to waive it, that is a red flag about the product itself. Always: (1) Take the full free-look period. (2) Read the full terms during this period. (3) Cancel if the product doesn't match what was promised.
negotiation_script: IRDAI mandates a 15-day free-look period that cannot be waived contractually. I'd like to exercise my statutory free-look rights regardless of what the 'special offer' terms say. Please process the cancellation.
indian_law_reference: IRDAI (Protection of Policyholders' Interests) Regulations, 2017 (free-look period); RBI Fair Practices Code

description: A 'free trial' that requires credit card details upfront and automatically converts to a paid subscription at the end of the trial period, with no reminder email, no confirmation, and a cancellation process that is intentionally difficult (hidden settings, phone-only, or long hold times).
why_risky: You sign up for a '7-day free trial,' forget about it, and discover months later that you've been charged ₹1,499/month. The cancellation button is hidden 5 clicks deep, and customer service puts you on hold for 45 minutes.
red_flag_phrases: free trial automatically converts to paid subscription
card will be charged at the end of the trial
cancel before the trial ends to avoid charges
no refunds for the first billing cycle
what_to_look_for: Check: (1) Does the trial require payment info upfront? (2) Will you receive a reminder before auto-conversion? (3) Is cancellation available online with one click? (4) Can you get a refund if you forgot to cancel? (5) Is the trial length clearly stated?
negotiation_leverage: Under the Consumer Protection Act 2019 and E-Commerce Rules 2020, consent for charges must be explicit and informed. Dark patterns that make cancellation difficult are explicitly prohibited under the CCPA's Dark Pattern Guidelines (2023).
safer_alternative: Ethical trial practices: (1) Trial without requiring payment info. (2) Email reminder 3 days before trial ends. (3) One-click cancellation available online. (4) If payment info is required, first charge only after explicit confirmation. (5) Pro-rata refund if cancelled within first billing cycle.
negotiation_script: I was not notified before the trial converted to a paid subscription, and the cancellation process is unreasonably difficult. Under the CCPA Dark Pattern Guidelines 2023, this is a prohibited practice. I'd like a full refund for all charges since the trial ended.
indian_law_reference: Consumer Protection Act, 2019; CCPA Guidelines for Prevention and Regulation of Dark Patterns, 2023

description: Terms of service that require all disputes to be resolved through the platform's own internal mechanism or through mandatory binding arbitration in a distant city, effectively preventing the consumer from approaching the Consumer Forum or civil courts.
why_risky: Consumer Forums are free, fast, and designed to protect you. By forcing disputes into arbitration (which is expensive) or the platform's own 'resolution center' (which is biased), the platform removes your most powerful remedy.
red_flag_phrases: all disputes shall be resolved through our internal mechanism
you agree to waive your right to class action
disputes subject to jurisdiction of courts in [distant city]
what_to_look_for: Check: (1) Does the TOS try to prevent you from going to Consumer Forum? (2) Is arbitration mandatory or optional? (3) Where is the arbitration venue? (4) Who pays the arbitration costs? (5) Is there a 'class action waiver'?
negotiation_leverage: The Supreme Court has consistently held that contractual arbitration clauses CANNOT oust the jurisdiction of Consumer Forums. Consumer Forums derive their jurisdiction from statute (CPA 2019), not contract. Any clause that attempts to waive Consumer Forum rights is void.
safer_alternative: Consumer Forum jurisdiction cannot be contractually waived. If you have a dispute: (1) File a complaint with the District Consumer Forum (for claims up to ₹1 crore). (2) Ignore the TOS arbitration clause — it cannot override your statutory rights. (3) File online at edaakhil.nic.in.
negotiation_script: Your terms require mandatory arbitration, but the Supreme Court has held that arbitration clauses cannot oust Consumer Forum jurisdiction. I intend to file a complaint with the Consumer Forum as is my statutory right under the Consumer Protection Act 2019.
indian_law_reference: Section 49, Consumer Protection Act, 2019 (jurisdiction); Supreme Court precedent — arbitration cannot oust Consumer Forum

description: A broadband or mobile data plan advertised as 'unlimited' but with a Fair Usage Policy (FUP) that throttles speeds to unusable levels (e.g., 256 kbps) after a certain data cap, without clearly disclosing the cap before purchase.
why_risky: You pay for an 'unlimited 100 Mbps' plan expecting full-speed unlimited data. After 500 GB (or less), your speed drops to 256 kbps — slower than 2G. The FUP was mentioned in fine print but the advertising prominently said 'unlimited.'
red_flag_phrases: unlimited data subject to Fair Usage Policy
after [X] GB, speeds may be reduced
what_to_look_for: Check: (1) What is the FUP data limit? (2) What speed do you get post-FUP? (Is 256 kbps even usable?) (3) Is the FUP clearly disclosed BEFORE purchase? (4) Can you upgrade or buy additional high-speed data? (5) Is 'unlimited' advertised prominently while FUP is in fine print?
negotiation_leverage: TRAI guidelines require ISPs to clearly disclose FUP limits at the point of sale. Advertising a plan as 'unlimited' while imposing severe speed restrictions can constitute misleading advertising under the Consumer Protection Act 2019. ASCI (Advertising Standards Council) has ruled against such practices.
safer_alternative: Choose plans that: (1) Clearly state the high-speed data cap upfront. (2) Offer reasonable post-FUP speeds (at least 2 Mbps). (3) Allow topping up with additional high-speed data. (4) Don't use 'unlimited' in marketing when there's a meaningful FUP cap.
negotiation_script: The plan was advertised as 'unlimited 100 Mbps' but speeds drop to 256 kbps after the FUP. This is misleading advertising. Under TRAI guidelines, the FUP should have been clearly disclosed. I'd like either a genuine unlimited plan or a proportionate reduction in charges.
indian_law_reference: TRAI Tariff Orders; Section 2(28), Consumer Protection Act, 2019 (misleading advertisement)

pattern_name: Credit Card Hidden Charges & Fee Escalation
description: Credit card terms that bury critical charges in a multi-page 'Most Important Terms and Conditions' (MITC) document — including annual fee increases, foreign transaction markups, cash advance fees, late payment charges, over-limit fees, and reward point devaluation.
why_risky: A 'zero annual fee' card may charge ₹5,000 from year 2. A '2% cashback' card may charge 3.5% on foreign transactions. These charges accumulate silently and can negate any rewards or benefits the card offers.
red_flag_phrases: annual fee waived for the first year
what_to_look_for: Check: (1) Is the annual fee truly zero or just waived for year 1? (2) What is the foreign transaction markup? (1.5-3.5% is common.) (3) What is the interest rate on unpaid balances? (36-42% p.a. is common in India.) (4) What happens if you only pay the 'minimum amount due'? (5) Can fees increase without notice?
negotiation_leverage: RBI Master Direction on Credit Cards (2022) mandates clear disclosure of all charges. Banks must provide the MITC before card issuance and cannot change fees without 30 days notice. You can request a fee waiver or card closure without charges.
safer_alternative: Before accepting a credit card: (1) Read the full MITC document. (2) Calculate the total annual cost (fees + typical interest). (3) Choose cards with lifetime-free offers. (4) Always pay the full amount due, not just the minimum. (5) Set up auto-pay for full payment.
negotiation_script: I'd like to review all charges including the annual fee from year 2, foreign transaction markup, and interest rates. Under the RBI Master Direction on Credit Cards, these must be clearly disclosed. Can you provide a complete fee schedule?
indian_law_reference: RBI Master Direction on Credit Card and Debit Card Issuance and Conduct, 2022; RBI Fair Practices Code
